items: Each data owner supplies raw counts early in the month (workforce, service delivery, complaints, incidents, audits/QIPs).
The Director of Quality validates each raw count against source documents (sample at least 3 entries per KPI).
Validated counts are entered; rates auto-calculate and RAG against target and baseline.

b.t: A dashboard is only as trustworthy as its validation — never enter a figure you have not traced to source.
The Executive Management Team pack pulls the month together into one governance view.

items: Sections: risk register, incidents & safeguarding, audits & QIPs, workforce, complaints, operations and finance.
Each section has a named owner and is due ~5 working days before the meeting.
The Director of Operations compiles and circulates the pack ~3 working days ahead and loads it to the QMS.

b.title: The complaints figure doesn’t match
b.situation: While validating the dashboard, the resolved-within-30-days rate from the return doesn’t match the Master Complaints Log.
b.action: Stop and reconcile before publishing. Trace both figures to source, find which is wrong (often a mis-dated closure), correct it, and only then enter the validated count. Note the anomaly so the return, dashboard and EMT pack all tell the same story.
b.why: The return, KPI dashboard and EMT pack must reconcile — auditors and the HSE cross-check them. Validation catches errors before they reach the Board.

quiz.q: A field on the return doesn’t apply this month. You should:
o: Leave it blank
Enter 0 or N/A
Guess a number
quiz.why: Never leave blanks — enter 0 or N/A so the return is complete and auditable.

quiz.q: Before a KPI figure goes on the dashboard it must be:
o: Estimated from memory
Validated against source documents (sample at least 3 per KPI)
Copied from last month
quiz.why: SOP-039: the Director of Quality validates each raw count against source before entry.

quiz.q: The return, KPI dashboard and EMT pack must:
o: Be produced by different people who never compare
Reconcile to the same underlying records
Only match at year end
quiz.why: All three reconcile to the same complaints, incident and roster records — auditors cross-check them.

oncall.title: On-call protocol & handover
oncall.summary: Run the on-call phone consistently — triage, follow the right pathway, log every call and hand over cleanly to the office.
objectives: Know on-call hours and the line
Triage a call for immediate risk
Follow the right pathway (missed visit, deterioration, safeguarding)
Log and hand over every call

b.t: On-call covers weekdays 17:00–08:30 and weekends 24 hours. The line is 01-416-3717.

items: Identify the caller and the issue: service user, family or carer? Clinical, safeguarding, staffing or environmental?
Check for immediate risk — life-threatening means 999/112 first.
Follow the right pathway for the issue type.

b.t: Every call is triaged the same way — make safe first, then route to the right pathway.

items: Life-threatening → 999/112 first, then continue.
Missed / late visit → confirm welfare and arrange cover (SOP-018).
Deterioration in condition → follow SOP-020; get clinical advice, do not attempt a clinical assessment.
Safeguarding → follow HS-23; incident → begin QA-13.
Record time, caller, issue and action taken for every call.
Brief the coordinator / CSM at the start of the next working day.

b.t: Every out-of-hours call must be logged and handed over — nothing waits silently until Monday.

b.title: A worried daughter rings at 10pm
b.situation: A Service User’s daughter calls the on-call line: her mother seems more confused than usual and is unsteady on her feet.
b.action: Triage: is there immediate danger (a fall, unresponsiveness)? If life-threatening, ensure 999/112. Otherwise treat as a possible deterioration (SOP-020): advise safety steps, arrange clinical advice (GP/out-of-hours doctor), and consider whether a visit is needed. Log the call with time, caller, observations and action, and hand over to the CSM first thing next morning.
b.why: On-call is structured triage, not diagnosis — make safe, route to the correct pathway, and never let a call go unlogged or un-handed-over.

quiz.q: The on-call line (01-416-3717) operates at weekends for:
o: 24 hours
Office hours only
quiz.why: Weekends are covered 24 hours; weekdays 17:00–08:30.

quiz.q: A caller reports a Service User seems to be deteriorating. You:
o: Do a quick clinical assessment yourself
Make safe, follow SOP-020 — arrange clinical advice; do not attempt a clinical assessment
Tell them to call their GP in the morning
quiz.why: On-call staff are non-clinical: make safe and route to clinical advice under SOP-020.

quiz.q: After handling any out-of-hours call you must:
o: Only log serious ones
Log it and hand over to the office next working day
Tell the next on-call if you remember
quiz.why: Every call is logged and formally handed over — nothing waits until Monday.

emergency.title: Out-of-hours emergency response
emergency.summary: Respond safely to the most common time-critical situations — a fall, a deterioration, a suspected infection — and protect lone workers.
objectives: Triage for immediate risk
Follow the fall / deterioration / infection pathways
Protect lone workers (HS-16)
Log and escalate correctly

b.t: Falls are among the most common emergencies. Safety and “do not move” come first.

items: Stay calm; do NOT move the Service User if injury is suspected (fracture, soft-tissue, head strike) or if they cannot get up.
Talk to them — ask what happened and whether they hit their head; reassure them.
If injury is suspected or they cannot get up, call 999/112 and keep them warm.
Notify the CSM / on-call by phone; inform the family; complete a QA-13 incident report within 3 working days.

b.t: HCAs are non-clinical — the job is to observe, make safe, and get clinical help fast.

items: Deterioration: ensure safety, note objective observations (breathing, colour, responsiveness), phone the on-call/CSM within ~5 minutes; 112/999 if life-threatening; send the home folder & medication list if hospitalised.

Suspected infection: apply Standard Precautions and the Point-of-Care Risk Assessment (PCRA card); report to the CSM who arranges GP/PHN assessment; 2+ linked cases = possible outbreak — notify the Director of Quality within 24 hours.

b.t: Deterioration leading to hospital admission is at minimum a Category 2 incident — open a QA-13 report.

lessons.t: Protect the lone worker & in practice

b.t: Carers work alone in the community, often after hours — their safety matters too.

items: If a carer is at risk, follow the Lone Worker Policy (HS-16); keep contact until they are safe and escalate if you cannot reach them.

b.title: An unwitnessed fall with a head strike
b.situation: A carer arrives to find the Service User on the floor; they say they “bumped their head” getting up and feel dizzy.
b.action: Do not move them. Because a head strike and dizziness are present, ensure 999/112 is called and keep them warm and still. Phone the on-call/CSM, inform the family, and stay until help arrives. Complete the QA-13 incident report the same shift — an unwitnessed fall with a head injury is likely Category 1/2 and is reviewed.
b.why: Head injury + unwitnessed fall = do not move, call emergency services, and report as an incident. Guessing they are “fine” is never the call to make.

quiz.q: A Service User has fallen and may have hit their head. You:
o: Help them up quickly to a chair
Do not move them, call 999/112, keep them warm and report it
Wait to see if they feel better
quiz.why: SOP-004: if injury/head strike is suspected, do not move them — call emergency services and complete a QA-13 report.

quiz.q: A deterioration leads to hospital admission. The incident is at least:
o: Not an incident
Category 2
Category 4
quiz.why: SOP-020: deterioration leading to hospital admission is at minimum a Category 2 incident.

quiz.q: If a lone carer is at risk out of hours you follow:
o: The complaints policy
The Lone Worker Policy (HS-16)
Nothing until Monday
quiz.why: Lone-worker risk is managed under HS-16.

continuity.title: Business continuity & call-down
continuity.summary: Activate the Business Continuity Plan during disruption — weather, pandemic or staffing shortage — protecting critical care and communicating clearly.
objectives: Know when and how the BCP is activated
Understand the Crisis Command roles
Prioritise critical Service Users and run the call-down
Communicate with staff, families and the HSE

b.t: The Business Continuity Plan addresses events such as Red Weather Warnings, pandemics and industrial action.

items: The CEO convenes Crisis Command: CEO as Incident Lead (strategy), Director of Operations coordinating the response.
Retrieve the Business Continuity Folder from the off-site secure location (Directory of Contacts, priority lists, call-down rota).
Prioritise continuity of care for high-priority / high-dependency Service Users first.
Deploy the skeleton-staff rota and reassign HCAs to priority calls.

b.t: Clear, constant communication is what holds a continuity response together.

items: Keep in regular contact with Service Users and families throughout the event.
Cascade to on-duty staff (email/phone/group) and off-duty staff (SMS/phone).
The CEO notifies HSE Emergency Management and, where relevant, insurers.
Maintain the Emergency Event Log of actions and decisions throughout.

b.t: The call-down list only works if it is current — it is checked as part of BCP readiness, not on the day of the crisis.

b.title: A Status Red storm overnight
b.situation: A Status Red wind warning is issued for tomorrow morning; travel is unsafe until midday.
b.action: The CEO activates the BCP and convenes Crisis Command. The Business Continuity Folder is retrieved and Service Users are prioritised — P1 medication and high-dependency calls first. A skeleton rota of reachable staff is deployed for essential visits; all affected Service Users and families are phoned proactively; HSE Emergency Management is notified; and every decision goes in the Emergency Event Log.
b.why: Continuity planning protects the most dependent people first and keeps everyone informed — improvising without the plan is how vulnerable Service Users get missed.

b.t: A continuity event is not over when the weather clears — there is a controlled return to normal and a learning step.

items: The CEO / Director of Operations formally stands the response down once it is safe to resume normal service.
Confirm every priority Service User has been seen or accounted for — chase any missed calls and log them as incidents where needed (QA-13).
Restore the normal roster and notify staff, Service Users and families that service has resumed.
Hold a short debrief and update the Business Continuity Folder and call-down list with anything that was out of date.

b.t: The best time to fix a wrong number or a stale priority list is in the debrief — while the event is fresh.

quiz.q: During a Red Weather Warning, you prioritise:
o: All visits equally
High-dependency and high-priority (P1) packages first
Only visits that are convenient
quiz.why: SOP-030: continuity protects the most dependent Service Users first.

quiz.q: Who leads the Crisis Command structure when the BCP is activated?
o: The on-call HCA
The CEO as Incident Lead, with the Director of Operations coordinating
Whoever is in the office
quiz.why: SOP-030: the CEO is Incident Lead; the Director of Operations coordinates the response.

quiz.q: Once the disruption passes, the continuity response is closed by:
o: Everyone just going back to normal
A formal stand-down: confirm every priority Service User was accounted for, restore the roster, and debrief to update the plan
Deleting the Emergency Event Log
quiz.why: SOP-030: there is a controlled stand-down — account for priority Service Users, resume normal service, and learn from the event.

supervision.title: Staff supervision & support
supervision.summary: Run planned, protected, confidential one-to-one supervision — at least quarterly — that monitors performance, closes competency gaps and supports wellbeing.
objectives: Know the supervision frequency and setup
Structure a supervision session
Record it and agree SMART actions
Link supervision to competence and wellbeing

items: Hold supervision on schedule — it is a safety and quality control, not a formality.
Balance support and accountability; listen as well as direct.
Link supervision to the NCCA and training plan.

b.t: Supervision is planned, protected and confidential — not a corridor chat.

items: New HCAs get one-to-one supervision across their first 8 hours; thereafter at least quarterly (more often for complex cases or concerns).
Hold it in private and uninterrupted; confirm confidentiality and its limits.
Review progress since last time, praise good practice, and work through problems and Service-User feedback.
Agree SMART actions, set the next date, and both sign and date the Supervision Record (stored securely in the staff file).
Watch for signs of stress or burnout; offer support early.
Reinforce professional boundaries (HR-38).
Link actions to the NCCA and the individual training plan.

b.title: A carer seems to be struggling
b.situation: In supervision an experienced HCA is quieter than usual and mentions they are finding one complex Service User “a lot” lately.
b.action: Keep the session private and unhurried. Listen, acknowledge, and explore the pressure without judgement. Agree practical supports — additional training, a shadowing refresh, a case discussion with the Clinical Lead, or workload adjustment — as SMART actions with a review date, and record them. Offer wellbeing supports and reinforce boundaries.
b.why: Supervision is a safety control as well as support: catching strain early protects both the carer and the Service User, and turns a worry into an owned action.

quiz.q: Formal supervision is held at minimum:
o: Annually
Quarterly (more often for complex cases or concerns)
Only when there is a problem
quiz.why: SOP-014: at least quarterly, with new HCAs supervised across their first 8 hours.

quiz.q: A supervision session should be:
o: A quick corridor catch-up
Planned, private and confidential, with a signed Supervision Record
Recorded only if something is wrong
quiz.why: SOP-014: supervision is planned, protected and confidential, and both parties sign the record.

quiz.q: Supervision should be linked to:
o: Nothing else
The NCCA and the individual training plan
Only pay reviews
quiz.why: Supervision connects to competency (NCCA) and the training plan.

careplan.title: Care plan oversight & review
careplan.summary: Assure care plans are person-centred, clinically signed off, current, and responsive to change — scheduled and triggered reviews.
objectives: Assure person-centred, consent-led planning
Keep scheduled reviews on time
Run a triggered review within timeframe

b.t: The care plan is built with the Service User and reflects their goals, not just their tasks.

items: Developed with the Service User / family; presume capacity and provide decision-making supports; apply FREDA.
Environmental (App 8), Falls (App 9) and Medication (App 10) risk assessments in place, each with a Risk Management Plan.
Getting to Know Me (App 7) captured; the Care Plan (App 6) signed off by the Clinical Lead.
A copy in the home folder and the office file (IM-30), accessible to all staff delivering care.

b.t: Reviews keep the plan honest and current.

items: Review at 6 weeks, 6 months, then annually — earlier if needs change.
Evidence Service User involvement in every review.
Confirm visits are actually delivered in line with the plan, including the minimum call length.

b.t: An unscheduled review is required when something changes — a fall, hospital discharge, deterioration or safeguarding pattern.

items: Capture the trigger in the CRM the same working day; involve the Clinical Lead for any clinical risk.
Initiate the review within 48 hours and complete it within 10 working days.
Update the risk assessments and Risk Management Plan; amend the plan only with consent and Clinical Lead sign-off.
Issue written notification of the outcome to the Service User / representative within 10 working days; raise a QIP (SOP-037) or add a risk (SOP-043) where a pattern emerges.

b.t: For HSE-funded Service Users, any change to the level or type of support goes to the HSE for review and decision.

quiz.q: Scheduled care-plan reviews happen at:
o: Annually only
6 weeks, then 6 months, then annually
Every 2 years
quiz.why: SOP-002: reviews at 6 weeks, 6 months and annually — earlier if needs change.

quiz.q: A triggered (unscheduled) review must be initiated within 48 hours and completed within:
o: 10 working days
28 days
3 months
quiz.why: SOP-032: initiate within 48 hours of the trigger and complete within 10 working days.

quiz.q: Changes to a care plan are signed off by:
o: The coordinator alone
The Clinical Lead
The HCA on the next visit
quiz.why: SOP-002 / SOP-032: care-plan changes require Clinical Lead sign-off, with Service User consent.

audit.title: Participating in quality audits
audit.summary: Understand how audits are planned and conducted, how to take part constructively, and how findings turn into improvement.
objectives: Understand the audit lifecycle
Know how an audit is planned & scheduled (SOP-034)
Know how an audit is conducted (SOP-035)
Act on findings

b.t: Audits follow a standard lifecycle: plan → scope → sample → conduct → report → QIP → close-out / re-audit → share learning.

items: Audits are evidence-based and anonymised in reporting.
Sampling reflects high and low levels of support.
Findings drive Quality Improvement Plans, not blame.

b.t: The annual Quality Audit Schedule is risk-based and approved before the year starts.

items: The Director of Quality maps the audit universe to HSE Specs, KPIs, the National Standards themes and risk.
The schedule is drafted on the LLH Audit Calendar, with auditors independent of the area they audit.
It is approved by the Director of Operations and submitted to the HSE by end of January.

b.t: Every audit uses the Audit Working Pack so it is consistent, evidence-based and fair.

items: The auditor agrees an Audit Plan and does a pre-audit document review.
An opening meeting, a walk-around, staff interviews and a random record sample follow.
Everything is captured on the Audit Trail Record; findings are good practice, non-conformances or observations.

b.title: Your area is being audited next week
b.situation: As a CSM you are the Audit Owner for a clinical audit and feel nervous about “being caught out.”
b.action: Engage with it: the auditor agrees a plan and scope with you in advance, reviews documents, and samples records anonymously. Your job is to give honest access and evidence — not to prepare a perfect show. Findings (including good practice) feed a QIP, not blame, and you help shape the corrective actions.
b.why: Audit is assurance and improvement, not inspection theatre. Independence, evidence and a no-blame culture are exactly what make findings trustworthy and useful.

quiz.q: Audit findings are used primarily to:
o: Blame individuals
Drive Quality Improvement Plans and shared learning
Satisfy paperwork only
quiz.why: Audits are improvement-focused and systems-based, not about blame.

quiz.q: An auditor should be:
o: The manager of the area being audited
Independent of the activity being audited (SOP-034)
Any available HCA
quiz.why: SOP-034: auditors are allocated to ensure independence from the activity being audited.

quiz.q: Service-user details in an audit report are:
o: Named in full
Anonymised
Left to the auditor
quiz.why: All details are anonymised in the Quality Audit Report.

qip.title: Managing QIP actions
qip.summary: Open a Quality Improvement Plan from a finding, drive SMART actions to completion, and close it out on evidence — with the right governance visibility.
objectives: Open and scope a QIP (SOP-037)
Write SMART, owned, risk-scored actions
Track and close on evidence (SOP-038)
Understand the governance reporting route

b.t: A QIP can start from an audit finding, an inspection, a complaint, feedback or an incident trend.

items: The Director of Quality allocates a unique QIP number and creates the record within 2 working days of the trigger.
A QIP Owner is assigned — by default the Audit Owner where it arose from an audit.
The QIP Owner risk-scores it (HSE matrix) as High/Medium/Low; High auto-notifies the Director of Operations.
Corrective actions are defined within 10 working days — each SMART with a named owner and target date.

b.t: An action without an owner and a date is a wish, not a plan.
QIPs are only closed when the action is done AND shown to have worked.

items: The QIP Log is the single source of truth (status, severity, due date, owner).
The Director of Quality issues a fortnightly prompt to owners with overdue or imminent actions.
The owner submits evidence of completion; High-risk QIPs may trigger a re-audit to confirm effectiveness.
Closure is recorded with the evidence reference and an effectiveness note; open QIPs are reported in the EMT & CGC packs.

b.title: An overdue medication QIP
b.situation: A QIP to roll out a revised medication record is two weeks past its target date and the owner has gone quiet.
b.action: The Director of Quality’s fortnightly prompt flags it as overdue. Contact the owner for a status and blocker, re-agree a realistic date (recording the change), and escalate to the Director of Operations because it is a High-risk clinical action. It stays Open until evidence of completion — and, given the risk, a re-audit — confirms it worked.
b.why: A QIP is not closed by good intentions or a passed deadline. Tracking, escalation and evidence of effectiveness are what turn a finding into real, lasting change.

quiz.q: A QIP action should always be:
o: A general aim
SMART with a named owner and a target date
Owned by everyone
quiz.why: SOP-037: each corrective action is SMART with a named owner and target completion date.

quiz.q: Before a QIP is closed, the Director of Quality must:
o: Assume it worked once the date passes
Review evidence of completion and effectiveness (re-audit if High-risk)
Wait for the annual review
quiz.why: SOP-038: QIPs close on evidence of completion and effectiveness, not on the deadline passing.

quiz.q: Open QIPs are reported to governance via:
o: Nowhere — they are internal
The monthly EMT pack and quarterly CGC pack
Only the annual report
quiz.why: SOP-038: open QIP status is reported in the EMT (monthly) and CGC (quarterly) packs for Board oversight.

triage.title: Remote triage & prioritisation
triage.summary: The decision framework for everything that comes in by phone — how to work out what it is, how urgent it is, who owns it, and when to escalate immediately.
objectives: Ask the one question that comes before all others
Sort a call into safety, clinical or operational — and by urgency
Route each call to the right owner the first time
Recognise the calls that must be escalated immediately, not queued

b.t: Triage is deciding, quickly and calmly, what a call actually is and what has to happen next. Before anything else you establish whether anyone is at risk right now.

items: Always open with the safety question: “Is anyone in immediate danger or hurt right now?”
If yes — 999/112 first, stay on the line, and only then continue. A life-safety call is never put on hold or queued.
If no — you have time to gather the facts calmly and decide the right route.

b.t: Never let uncertainty delay a safety response. If you are unsure whether someone is at risk, treat it as if they are until you know otherwise.

lessons.t: Sort it: what kind of call is this?

b.t: Once safety is established, every call sorts into one of three lanes. The lane decides who owns it.

items: Safety / safeguarding — a disclosure, suspected abuse, a fall, an injury, a medication error. Owner: DSO (safeguarding) or Clinical Lead / CSM (clinical), the same day.
Clinical — a change in the person, a care concern, a medication query. Owner: Clinical Lead / CSM.
Operational — a late or missed visit, a carer off sick, a roster change, a general query. Owner: you and the coordinator team, with the CSM for anything you cannot cover.

b.t: Write the lane down as you decide it — it becomes the reason code and lets the office see patterns across calls.
Within each lane, urgency decides the timeframe. Use a simple three-level scale.

steps: Immediate — danger, serious harm, a safeguarding disclosure or a Category 1 incident. Act now; escalate before you hang up.
Same day — a missed visit, a fall with no obvious injury, a complaint, a clinical change. Log, act and notify the owner today.
Routine — a future roster change, a general enquiry, non-urgent feedback. Log and route; no same-day pressure.

b.t: When two things compete, protect the highest-risk service user first — continuity and safety beat convenience every time.

lessons.t: Route it — and don’t drop it

b.t: A call is only triaged when it has an owner and a next step. Your job is to make sure nothing sits silently.

items: Name the owner and the timeframe out loud to the caller: who will act, and by when.
Log it the same shift with the reason code and the action you took.
If it is out of hours, follow the on-call route — don’t leave it for Monday.
Close the loop: if you promised a call back, make sure it happens or is handed over.

b.title: Two calls at once
b.situation: A family rings about a carer running 40 minutes late, and on the other line a carer reports the service user has had a fall.
b.action: Take the fall first — ask the safety question, arrange the clinical response and raise the incident. Reassure the late-visit caller you’ll call straight back, arrange cover, and log both.
b.why: Urgency and risk decide order, not who called first. The fall is a same-day clinical/safety event; the late visit is important but lower-risk.

quiz.q: The first thing to establish on any incoming call is:
o: Which carer is involved
Whether anyone is in immediate danger right now
Whether it needs a reason code
quiz.why: Safety comes before everything. If anyone is at risk it is 999/112 first, then continue.

quiz.q: A service user has had a fall with no obvious injury. This is:
o: Routine — log it for the weekly review
Same day — log, act and notify the Clinical Lead/CSM today, and raise an incident
Immediate only if they ask for an ambulance
quiz.why: A fall is a same-day clinical/safety event: incident raised (QA-13) and the owner notified the same day, even with no visible injury.

quiz.q: A call is properly triaged only when:
o: You have written down what was said
It has a named owner and a next step with a timeframe
You have reassured the caller
quiz.why: Triage means routed and owned — nothing is triaged until someone owns it with a clear next step and timeframe.

quiz.q: Two calls come at once: a late visit and a reported fall. You:
o: Take them in the order they rang
Handle the fall first, then call the late-visit caller straight back
Put both on hold and ask the CSM
quiz.why: Risk decides order. The fall is a same-day safety/clinical event; reassure and call the lower-risk caller straight back.

difficultcalls.title: Handling difficult & high-emotion calls
difficultcalls.summary: Stay calm and in control when a caller is angry, distressed, grieving or disclosing something serious — de-escalate, keep everyone safe, and still capture what matters.
objectives: Steady a heated or distressed call with a clear technique
Take a complaint or a safeguarding disclosure by phone correctly
Hold professional boundaries and end an abusive call safely
Look after yourself after a hard call

b.t: People ring at their worst moments — frightened, angry, grieving. Your calm is the most useful thing in the conversation. It is almost never about you personally.

items: Lower your pace and your volume — do not match their heat.
Let them finish before you respond; interrupting adds fuel.
Name what you hear: “I can hear how worried you are — let’s sort this together.”
Focus on the next concrete step you can take, not on winning the point.

b.t: A simple sequence: Listen → Acknowledge → Apologise for the experience → Act. You can acknowledge someone’s upset without admitting fault.
A complaint is a gift — it tells you where the service fell short. Never defend at the point of contact.

items: Open with: “I’m really sorry to hear that — thank you for telling me.”
Resolve it there if you genuinely can; otherwise take the full details.
Explain the next step honestly: the Complaints Officer will be in touch within 5 working days.
Log it on the Master Complaints Log (QA-03) with a verbatim record where it matters.

b.t: Never argue, minimise or promise an outcome you can’t guarantee. If a complaint hints at abuse or neglect, treat it as safeguarding and route to the DSO the same day.
Sometimes the hardest calls are the quietest — someone telling you something is wrong at home. How you respond in the first minute matters enormously.

items: Listen, stay calm, take it seriously and use their own words.
Reassure them — but never promise to keep it secret.
Do not investigate, do not ask leading questions, do not contact the alleged person.
Record exactly what was said and report to the DSO the same day.

b.t: You receive and route a disclosure — you never investigate it. Anything more than listening and recording can damage a future safeguarding process.

lessons.t: Boundaries, safety and after the call

b.t: You can be kind and firm at the same time. You do not have to accept abuse, and you should not carry a hard call alone.

items: If a caller becomes abusive: stay calm and set one clear boundary — “I want to help, but I can’t continue if the language continues.”
If it continues, tell them you are ending the call and that someone will follow up, then end it and log it.
Never give personal contact details or take on something outside your role (professional boundaries, HR-38).
After a distressing call, tell your supervisor — debrief, record it, and take a moment. This is support, not weakness.

b.title: An angry son on the line
b.situation: A service user’s son is shouting that the morning carer never showed and threatens to “report you all”.
b.action: Let him finish, acknowledge the failure and apologise for the experience, establish the missed visit and arrange cover now, log it as a complaint and a missed-visit incident, and tell him the Complaints Officer will be in touch within 5 working days.
b.why: Meeting anger with calm and concrete action de-escalates it and turns a threat into a logged, owned complaint the service can actually put right.

quiz.q: A caller is shouting and upset. Your first move is to:
o: Match their urgency so they know you care
Lower your pace and volume, let them finish, and acknowledge their feeling
Tell them to calm down or you’ll end the call
quiz.why: Calm de-escalates; matching their heat escalates. Listen, acknowledge, then move to the next concrete step.

quiz.q: Someone discloses possible abuse and asks you to keep it secret. You:
o: Agree, so they keep trusting you
Reassure them but explain you must pass it to the DSO to keep them safe
Ask them who did it and exactly what happened
quiz.why: Never promise secrecy and never investigate. Receive it, record their words, and report to the DSO the same day.

quiz.q: The right opening for a complaint on the phone is:
o: To explain the likely reason it wasn’t our fault
“I’m really sorry to hear that — thank you for telling me.”
To transfer them to a manager immediately
quiz.why: Acknowledge and apologise for the experience, take the details, and log it (QA-03) — never defend at the point of contact.

quiz.q: A caller becomes abusive and won’t stop after you set a boundary. You should:
o: Keep taking it — the customer is always right
Tell them you’re ending the call, that someone will follow up, then end it and log it
Hang up without a word
quiz.why: You don’t have to accept abuse. Set one clear boundary, and if it continues, end the call safely, explain follow-up, and record it.

crmlogging.title: Logging it right in the CRM
crmlogging.summary: The record discipline behind every call — what to capture, in whose words, with the right reason code, protecting personal data, and handing over so nothing is lost.
objectives: Capture the six things every call record needs
Use reason codes so the office can see patterns
Protect personal data on the phone and in the record
Hand over cleanly so nothing waits silently

lessons.t: If it isn’t written down, it didn’t happen

b.t: The record is the service’s memory. A good log lets the next person act without ringing the caller back, and stands up months later in an audit or a complaint.

items: Log every call the same shift — not from memory at the end of the week.

Capture: who called and their relationship to the service user; the service user’s name; date and time; what was said; the action you took and who you routed it to; and what you committed to, with the timeframe.
Record facts, not opinions. For a complaint or a disclosure, use the caller’s own words in quotation marks.

b.t: Write the record as if the person you’re handing to has never heard the story — because often they haven’t.

lessons.t: Reason codes turn calls into patterns

b.t: The reason code is what lets the office see the signal in the noise — three “late visit” calls in one area this week is an early warning, not a coincidence.

items: Always set the reason code that matches the lane — safety, clinical or operational, and the specific type.
Be consistent: the same kind of call gets the same code every time.
Flag clusters to the CSM — a pattern across calls is a risk the individual calls don’t show.

b.title: The cluster hiding in plain sight
b.situation: By Thursday you’ve logged three separate “carer running late” calls in the same town.
b.action: Handle each properly, but because you coded them consistently you can see the pattern — flag the cluster to the CSM and Operations as an emerging risk.
b.why: Consistent coding turns a pile of individual calls into a pattern the service can act on before it becomes a serious incident or a complaint.

lessons.t: Protect the data (GDPR on the phone)

b.t: A service user’s information is special-category personal data. The record must be accurate, relevant and shared only with people who are authorised.

items: Verify the caller’s identity and authority before discussing or recording anything sensitive.
Keep the record factual and relevant — don’t write things that aren’t needed.
Keep safeguarding detail out of the open CRM — file it to the restricted safeguarding record only.
If you disclose to the wrong person, report it to the DPO immediately as a personal-data breach.

b.t: Sharing a service user’s details with someone not authorised on the file is a data breach — report it to the DPO the same day (dpo@libertyhomecare.ie).

lessons.t: Hand over so nothing is lost

b.t: Your shift ends; the service user’s needs don’t. A clean handover is the difference between continuity and a dropped ball.

items: At end of shift, brief the incoming coordinator / CSM on anything open or promised.
For out-of-hours calls, hand over to the office at the start of the next working day.
Make sure every promised call-back has an owner — don’t assume someone else saw it.
Check nothing you took today is sitting unrouted.

b.t: “Nothing waits silently” is the whole job — an open item with no owner is how things go wrong.

quiz.q: Every call record must include:
o: Only complaints and incidents
Who, when, what was said, the action taken and who it was routed to, and what you committed to
A summary written up at the end of the week
quiz.why: Log every call the same shift with who / when / what / action / owner / commitment — that record is what lets the office act and see patterns.

quiz.q: Why do reason codes matter?
o: They’re required by the software
They let the office see patterns across calls — a cluster is a risk a single call doesn’t show
They speed up typing
quiz.why: Consistent codes turn individual calls into patterns (e.g. a late-visit cluster) that can be acted on early.

quiz.q: A caller you can’t verify asks for a service user’s details. You:
o: Share it — they clearly know the person
Verify identity and authority first; if you can’t, take a message and check before disclosing
Refuse and hang up
quiz.why: Special-category data is only shared with authorised people. Verify first; a wrong disclosure is a breach reportable to the DPO.

quiz.q: At the end of your shift you have an open call-back you promised. You:
o: Leave it — someone will pick it up
Hand it over to the incoming coordinator / CSM with a clear owner
Only note it if it was urgent
quiz.why: Nothing waits silently — every open or promised item is handed over with a named owner so continuity holds.

oncalldecision.title: When to wake the on-call manager
oncalldecision.summary: A clear decision aid for out-of-hours — what always warrants calling the on-call manager, what can safely wait until morning, and how to brief them fast.
objectives: Know what must reach the on-call manager immediately
Judge what can safely wait for the next working day
Brief the on-call manager in under a minute
Keep lone workers safe out of hours

lessons.t: The golden rule out of hours

b.t: Out of hours there is no office to fall back on — the on-call manager is the safety net. When in doubt, call: it is always better to wake someone than to sit on a risk.

items: On-call line: 01-416-3717 — weekdays 17:00–08:30 and 24h at weekends.
If anyone is in immediate danger, 999/112 comes first, then the on-call manager.
Every out-of-hours call you take is logged and handed over — nothing waits silently until Monday.

b.t: If you are unsure whether something warrants waking on-call, it does. Escalate first and stand it down later — never the other way round.
These always warrant an immediate call to the on-call manager.

items: A safeguarding disclosure or suspected abuse.
A fall, injury, or a marked change in a service user.
A medication error, or a service user who is unwell.
A missed visit or an uncovered high-risk call with no cover.
A lone worker who is in trouble, threatened, or out of contact.
The death of a service user.
Any incident that looks Category 1 / serious.

b.t: Category 1 or safeguarding out of hours = call immediately, then follow the right pathway (QA-13 / HS-23).

lessons.t: These can usually wait for morning

b.t: Lower-risk items can be logged and handed to the office at the start of the next working day — no need to wake anyone.

items: A routine roster change for a future date.
A general enquiry with no safety element.
Non-urgent feedback or a compliment.
A supply or admin query that isn’t time-critical.

b.t: Log it clearly tonight so the morning team picks it up — “handed over” only counts if it’s written down.
When you do call, give them what they need to decide in one clear minute.

steps: Who and where — service user name, location, who else is involved.
What has happened — the facts, briefly, no opinions.
What you’ve done — 999? cover arranged? incident started?
What you need — a decision, or for them to take it on.

b.title: A carer can’t reach a service user at 8pm
b.situation: A carer rings: no answer at the door, curtains drawn, and the service user isn’t picking up the phone.
b.action: Treat it as a welfare concern — keep the carer safe (don’t force entry alone), call the on-call manager immediately, and be ready to call 999 / next of kin as they direct.
b.why: A possible collapse behind a locked door is a time-critical safety risk — it always warrants waking on-call, and the carer should never put themselves at risk.

quiz.q: You’re unsure whether something is serious enough to call on-call. You:
o: Wait until morning to be safe
Call — if you’re unsure it warrants a call; stand it down later if needed
Ask another coordinator first
quiz.why: When in doubt, escalate first and downgrade later — never sit on a possible risk out of hours.

quiz.q: Which of these can usually wait until the next working day?
o: A fall with no obvious injury
A routine roster change for next week
A carer who is out of contact
quiz.why: A future roster change is low-risk and handed over; a fall or a lost lone worker warrant an immediate call.

quiz.q: The on-call line and hours are:
o: The office mobile, 9–5
01-416-3717 — weekdays 17:00–08:30 and 24h weekends
Whoever answers first
quiz.why: On-call: 01-416-3717, covering weekday evenings/nights and full weekends.

quiz.q: A carer can’t get a response from a service user behind a locked door. You:
o: Tell the carer to force entry
Keep the carer safe, call on-call immediately, be ready for 999 / next of kin
Log it for the morning
quiz.why: A possible collapse behind a locked door is time-critical — wake on-call now and never ask a lone worker to put themselves at risk.

coordinatorshift.title: The coordinator’s day: start to handover
coordinatorshift.summary: The rhythm of a safe coordinator shift — the checks that open it, the sweeps that keep it safe through the day, and the handover that closes it so nothing is dropped.
objectives: Open the shift with the right checks
Run the sweeps that catch problems early
Keep the roster and the log honest all day
Hand over so nothing waits silently

b.t: The first half hour sets up a safe day. Before the calls build, know where the risks are.

items: Read the handover from the previous shift / on-call — pick up anything open or promised.
Scan today’s roster for gaps, unallocated visits and high-risk calls.
Check for carer call-outs and sort cover for the highest-risk service users first.
Note anything the office flagged — new referrals, service users of concern.

b.t: Five minutes reading the handover saves an hour of firefighting later.
Problems are cheap to fix early and expensive late. Build in regular sweeps rather than waiting for the phone to ring.

items: Missed-visit sweep — check the clock-ins; chase anything showing late (>30 min) before it becomes a missed visit (>60 min / non-delivery).
Message triage — clear voicemails, texts and emails so nothing sits unseen.
Roster integrity — keep continuity of carer where you can; never shorten a visit to fit.
Reason-code your calls as you go, and flag any cluster to the CSM.

b.t: A visit passing 60 minutes late, or not delivered, is a missed visit — reallocate by risk, tell the service user, and raise an incident (QA-13).
The service user’s needs don’t clock off with you. A clean handover is the whole point of the shift ending well.

items: List everything open or promised, with a named owner for each.
Brief the incoming coordinator / CSM — or the on-call manager if it’s out of hours.
Confirm every high-risk visit for the evening / next morning is covered.
Make sure nothing you took today is sitting unrouted or unlogged.

b.title: The half-finished callback
b.situation: It’s 5pm, you promised a family a call back about tomorrow’s carer, and you’ve run out of time.
b.action: Don’t leave it in your head — log it, and hand it explicitly to the incoming coordinator or on-call with the family’s number and what was promised.
b.why: A promise held only in your memory is a dropped ball waiting to happen; handed over with an owner, it’s continuity.

quiz.q: The first thing to do when you open a coordinator shift is:
o: Start answering the phone
Read the handover and scan the roster for gaps and high-risk calls
Make a coffee and wait for calls
quiz.why: Reading the handover and scanning the roster tells you where the risks are before the day builds.

quiz.q: A visit is now 65 minutes late with no delivery. This is:
o: Still just “late”
A missed visit — reallocate by risk, tell the service user, raise an incident
Only a problem if the family complains
quiz.why: >60 minutes or non-delivery = a missed visit, raised as an incident (QA-13) and covered by risk.

quiz.q: The point of the end-of-shift handover is:
o: To finish your notes
To pass every open or promised item to a named owner so nothing is dropped
To tell the manager you’re leaving
quiz.why: Handover exists so nothing waits silently — every open item leaves your shift with an owner.

quiz.q: You promised a callback but ran out of time. You:
o: Hope you remember tomorrow
Log it and hand it over with the number and what was promised
Leave a sticky note on your desk
quiz.why: A promise held only in memory gets dropped; logged and handed to a named owner, it holds.

pathways.focus: Safe, person-centred personal care in the home — rights, safety and knowing when to escalate
Scheduling, allocation, referral intake & family communication
Records, data entry, document control & HSE returns
Supervision, care oversight, complaints investigation & audit

SOP-001.title: Receive and Screen a New Service User Referral
SOP-001.purpose: To establish a consistent, auditable process for receiving, recording and screening every new Service User referral (HSE, private or other) so that suitability, prioritisation and initial allocation decisions are made without delay and in line with Liberty Living's HSE Authorisation Scheme obligations.

steps.action: Receive the referral and log it in the CRM within the same working day, capturing referral source, date received, Service User name, address, contact, HSE reference (if applicable) and presenting needs.

Confirm the referral route - HSE service request via Approved Provider portal, CDHS Service User selection, or private/other route - and acknowledge the HSE request within the HSE-specified response timeframe.
steps.role: Director of Operations / Client Services Manager
steps.tf: Per HSE Authorisation Scheme response window

steps.action: Conduct a preliminary phone call with the Service User or their representative to introduce Liberty Living, explain the assessment process, set expectations, and arrange the initial home visit.
Carry out a desktop screen against capacity criteria - geographical coverage, HCA availability, skills match, language/cultural fit, and any flagged safeguarding or complex-care indicators.

Where the referral cannot be accepted, confirm inability promptly back to the HSE (or referrer) in writing with reasons; where it can be accepted, confirm acceptance and provisional start arrangements.
Apply an initial priority indicator (P1 urgent, P2 standard, P3 routine) based on referral information available, pending the full assessment.
Open the Service User physical and electronic file in line with IM-30 Record Keeping, attach the referral documentation, and lock access to authorised staff only.
Hand over to the assessment workflow (SOP-002) by scheduling the comprehensive initial assessment to be completed within 7 working days of the HSE service request.
steps.tf: Within 7 working days of HSE request

SOP-002.title: Conduct an Initial Service User Assessment and Develop a Home Support Care Plan
SOP-002.purpose: To deliver a comprehensive, person-centred initial assessment of every new Service User and to produce a signed Home Support Care Plan that is rights-based, consent-led and aligned to the HSE referral, so that care delivered from day one is safe, appropriate and proportionate.

steps.action: Visit the Service User in their home and introduce Liberty Living, the Charter of Rights and Responsibilities, and the Service User Guide; provide information in a plain-English format the Service User can understand.
steps.tf: Within 7 working days of HSE service request

steps.action: Presume capacity. Provide all practicable supports to decision-making (advocacy, supporters, repeated information, accessible formats) and check for any existing Decision Support Arrangement (assistant, co-decision-maker, representative, AHD, EPA).

Seek informed consent for assessment and proposed care. Record consent in the Service User record (verbal, written or implied as appropriate). Obtain written consent for sharing of personal information (Appendix 3) and, if applicable, written Consent to Prompt Medication (Appendix 4).

Complete the comprehensive initial assessment using the Liberty Living Review checklist (Appendix 1): ADLs, communication needs, environmental risks, equipment, manual handling needs, medication needs, safeguarding indicators, and HSE prioritisation (P1/P2/P3).
steps.tf: Within 7 working days of HSE request

steps.action: Complete the Environmental Risk Assessment (Appendix 8), Falls Risk Assessment (Appendix 9), and Medication Risk Assessment (Appendix 10), and put a Risk Management Plan in place for each risk identified.
steps.tf: Within 7 working days of HSE request

steps.action: Complete the Getting to Know Me template (Appendix 7) capturing the Service User's preferences, life history, will, cultural and religious practices, advocacy needs and communication preferences — applying FREDA principles (Fairness, Respect, Equality, Dignity, Autonomy).
steps.tf: At first visit or within 7 working days

steps.action: Develop the personalised Home Support Care Plan (Appendix 6) reflecting Service User goals, agreed tasks, schedule, medication prompting plan (if any), and aligned to the HSE referral; sign the Care Agreement.
Submit the draft Home Support Care Plan to the Clinical Lead for clinical sign-off; incorporate any clinical amendments.
Communicate the Care Plan and Schedule to the allocated HCAs; place a copy in the Service User home folder and the internal office file (per IM-30).
Confirm service initiation to the HSE within 24 hours of the first care call (per GOV-25).
steps.role: Client Services Manager / Director of Operations

steps.action: Schedule the first review at 6 weeks, then 6 months, then annually; flag for earlier review if needs change.

SOP-003.title: Deliver a Medication Prompt
SOP-003.purpose: To set out the strict scope and step-by-step procedure for prompting a Service User to take their own medication. Liberty Living prompts. We do NOT administer. This SOP exists to keep Service Users safe and to keep HCAs operating squarely within their scope of practice.

steps.action: On arrival, check the Home Support Care Plan and the home-folder medication prompt schedule to confirm the time, the medicine(s) the Service User is due to self-take, and that written consent to prompt is on file.
steps.tf: At start of each care call

steps.action: Greet the Service User, confirm they are well, and ask whether they have already taken their medicines for that prompt time.

Prompt the Service User. Permitted prompting actions are limited to: telling/reminding the Service User of the time; reminding them to take/use their medicines; bringing the medicines (in their original packaging) to the Service User; ensuring the Service User has access to a drink.
steps.tf: At the prompt time in the Care Plan

steps.action: Allow the Service User to remove the medication from its packaging and take it themselves. The Service User retains responsibility for taking their own medication.
Log every prompt in the Service User record via the CRM the same shift — including time, medicine name as listed in the Care Plan, whether the Service User took it, missed it, or refused.
If a dose is missed or refused, report immediately by phone to the Supervisor/Client Services Manager and document the refusal and reason in the Service User record.

If the Service User shows any sign of a possible adverse drug reaction (rash, wheezing, swelling, difficulty breathing, dizziness, nausea, loss of consciousness), call 112/999 immediately, provide Basic Life Support if trained, notify Supervisor/Client Services Manager, stay with the Service User, and follow medical in
Raise a QA-13 incident report for any missed dose, refusal pattern, adverse drug reaction, packaging issue, or any deviation from the Care Plan.
steps.tf: Within 3 working days of occurrence; same day for ADR

steps.action: Where any capacity concern arises (Service User appears confused about their medication, repeated refusals, unsafe self-management), escalate to the Supervisor/Client Services Manager who escalates to the Clinical Lead per CARE-06.

SOP-004.title: Respond to a Fall
SOP-004.purpose: To give every HCA a clear, step-by-step response to follow when a Service User has fallen or falls during a care call — keeping the Service User safe, getting medical help when needed, and creating the documentation needed for review and learning.

steps.action: Stay calm. Do not move the Service User if there is any suspicion of injury (fracture, soft tissue injury, head strike) or if the Service User cannot move/get up themselves.
Speak to the Service User. Ask what happened, where it hurts, and whether they hit their head. Reassure them that help is on the way.
If injury is suspected or the Service User cannot get up, call 999 or 112 for emergency services. Keep the Service User warm with coats or blankets until help arrives. Do NOT attempt to lift.

If no injury is suspected and the Service User confirms they are well, follow CARE-32 Appendix 2 Post Fall Guidance to assist the Service User to a safe seated position where possible. Do not undertake any clinical assessment — that is for a healthcare professional.
Immediately notify the Line Manager / Client Services Manager by phone of the fall and current situation.
steps.tf: Same shift, within minutes of incident

steps.action: Immediately contact the Service User's representative (next of kin / family) to inform them of the fall, and advise them to inform the GP.

Complete a QA-13 incident report covering: run-up to the fall (slip/trip/near fall/unwitnessed); description of incident (state clearly if unwitnessed — record 'found on floor' rather than 'fell'); cause if known; immediate mitigating actions; nature of any injury or impact area.
steps.tf: Same shift, no later than 3 working days

steps.action: Stay with the Service User until family or emergency services arrive (where the Service User is alone and at risk).
On notification, the Client Services Manager reviews the Falls Risk Assessment and updates the Home Support Care Plan; HCAs are informed of any change to care delivery.

Where injury, deterioration, or repeated/unexplained/suspicious falls are identified, refer to GP/PHN/healthcare professional without delay, and consider escalation as a safeguarding concern under HS-23.1 (SOP-005).
External reporting (HSE/HIQA where applicable) managed in line with QA-13 Section 7.11 and GOV-36.

SOP-005.title: Receive an Adult Safeguarding Disclosure
SOP-005.purpose: To set out exactly what every Liberty Living staff member must do when a Service User discloses abuse, when abuse is observed, or when a third party reports a safeguarding concern. The Service User's safety is paramount. Liberty Living has zero tolerance for abuse.

steps.action: DO listen to the Service User. Take what they say seriously. Believe them. Use the disclosure-handling principles in HS-23.1 Appendix 1 — be calm, do not interrupt, do not lead, use the Service User's own words.

DO take immediate safety actions to safeguard anyone at immediate risk of harm — call 999/112 for medical assistance or An Garda Siochana if a criminal act may have occurred or is about to occur. Do not delay safety actions to complete paperwork.

DO preserve physical evidence where required. Leave things as and where they are; handle to the absolute minimum; do not clean up; do not wash anything; do not interfere with clothing/footwear; in sexual assault cases preserve bedding and clothing and avoid physical contact with the victim or alleged perpetrator beyond

DO NOT promise confidentiality or secrecy to the Service User. Be honest that the information must be shared with the Line Manager / DSO so that the Service User can be protected. Reassure the Service User that they will be supported.
DO NOT investigate. Do not interview the Service User or other witnesses. Do not confront or warn the alleged abuser. Do not discuss the disclosure with anyone other than your Line Manager / DSO.

Report the concern immediately to the Line Manager (or, in their absence, directly to the DSO or CEO). The Line Manager immediately escalates to the DSO, who notifies the CEO. Out-of-hours: report to the on-call coordinator and escalate to the DSO at start of next working day.
steps.role: Staff member -> Line Manager -> DSO -> CEO

steps.action: Complete the Incident Report Form (Microsoft Forms) on the same day, with the support of the DSO/Line Manager if needed. Be factual, use the Service User's own words, keep it legible, signed and dated. Include: when, who was involved, what was said/seen, any other relevant information.
Store the Incident Report Form confidentially in a secure location until needed. Do not share it beyond the safeguarding chain.
DSO conducts an immediate risk assessment to determine ongoing risk to the Service User, checks what steps have been taken, and instigates any further safeguarding actions required.

DSO contacts the Service User's family/representative or appointed Decision Supporter where appropriate (taking account of the Service User's wishes and capacity); where a Decision Supporter is appointed, they must be informed immediately.
DSO initiates Open Disclosure within 24-48 hours of the allegation per QA-13 (SOP-010) where applicable.

DSO reports the concern to the HSE Safeguarding and Protection Team (Vulnerable Persons) within 3 working days, or immediately if urgent, and to the HSE nominated point of contact as soon as possible. Where a criminal act is suspected, agreement on engagement with the alleged perpetrator is discussed with An Garda Sioc
Following Day 1 activities, initiate the Preliminary Screening process per SOP-007.

SOP-006.title: Receive a Child Protection Concern
SOP-006.purpose: To ensure Liberty Living staff respond promptly, factually and lawfully when they have a concern, observation or disclosure relating to the safety or welfare of a child or young person - escalating through the Designated Liaison Person (DLP) and, where reasonable grounds for concern exist, reporting to Tusla under the Children First Act 2015.

steps.action: Prioritise the safety and wellbeing of the child. Ensure the child is not in immediate physical danger. Where there is immediate or serious risk, call An Garda Siochana (999/112) who can bring the child to a place of safety and access Tusla's Out of Hours Social Work Service.
DO NOT promise confidentiality or anonymity. Be honest with the child/Service User/parent that the information must be shared with Tusla where there is a child protection or welfare concern.
Where a disclosure is made by a child/young person, respond with sensitivity and professionalism (HS-28 Appendix 3). Listen. Use the child's own words. Do not lead or interpret.
DO NOT investigate. Do not interview the child further, the parents, or the alleged perpetrator. Do not warn the alleged perpetrator. Liberty Living does not investigate - Tusla does.
Report the concern immediately to the DLP / Deputy DLP (on/off-site, contactable 24/7). The DLP reports the matter to the relevant Mandated Person.

Document the concern in writing the same day, completed within 24 hours: typed where possible; 24-hour clock and dated; signed with full name in block capitals; factual; use the child/adult's own words; record any colleagues who may have witnessed; identify third-party information clearly.

Mandated Person and/or DLP review the concern against the 'reasonable grounds for concern' criteria (specific indication by the child; account by witness; injury/behaviour consistent with abuse; consistent emotional/physical neglect; any concern about possible sexual abuse).
steps.tf: Same day or next working day

steps.action: Where applicable and safe to do so, the DLP/Mandated Person speaks to the parent/guardian and explains the report received, seeking clarification - UNLESS this would put the child or the reporter at further risk. If parents are not informed, the reasons are clearly recorded.

Where reasonable grounds for concern are met, the Mandated Person completes the Tusla Child Protection and Welfare Report Form (CPWRF) and submits via the Tusla portal (https://portal.tusla.ie/) as soon as practicable. Mandated Person may submit jointly with the DLP. A Mandated Person's statutory obligation cannot be d
steps.tf: As soon as practicable; written report within 3 days if verbal made first

steps.action: Where there is uncertainty about reasonable grounds, the Mandated Person/DLP contacts the Tusla Dedicated Contact Point for advice. Where the decision is not to report, document the concern and reasons in full, advise the person who raised the concern in writing, and inform them they may consult Tusla or An Garda Sioch
steps.tf: Same day or next working day

steps.action: DLP continues to monitor the situation, works in conjunction with Tusla, and updates Tusla whenever relevant. Where a concern was identified about a staff member, the Trust in Care procedure within HS-23.1 also applies.

SOP-007.title: Conduct Safeguarding Preliminary Screening
SOP-007.purpose: To ensure that every allegation, disclosure or observation of abuse is screened by the DSO (in conjunction with the Clinical Lead) within the required timeframe to establish whether an abusive act could have occurred and whether reasonable grounds for concern exist, so that the right next step - Safeguarding Plan, internal/external inquiry, or HSE Safeguarding & Protection Team management - is selected.

steps.action: DSO, in conjunction with the Clinical Lead, reviews the Incident Report Form and all readily available information within 24 hours of being notified. Throughout, the welfare of the vulnerable Service User remains paramount.

Establish the basic facts on the PSF1: what is the allegation; who is reporting; who is involved; what actions have been taken to date; biographical information of those involved; what is known of capacity and wishes; immediate risks; current safety status of the victim; whether An Garda Siochana have been notified.

At any stage where there is concern that a serious criminal offence may have been or is about to be committed, contact An Garda Siochana immediately. Do not compromise their statutory responsibilities.

Investigate the nature of the concern: timeframe, any assessments already completed, any other responses, and identify any previous safeguarding concerns recorded against this Service User and/or alleged perpetrator. Where doubt exists, seek guidance from the local HSE Safeguarding and Protection Team or National Safeg
Carry out a risk assessment and a staffing review as part of the screening, to ensure the safety of staff who may be at risk.
Capture the views and wishes of the affected Service User on the screening record, taking account of capacity and any Decision Supporter arrangement.

Reach one of three preliminary screening outcomes and document on the Preliminary Screening Outcome Form (PSF2): (a) no grounds for reasonable concerns exist; (b) additional information required; (c) reasonable grounds for concern exist.

Where 'no grounds' is the outcome: record the decision on the staff personnel file (where staff member is involved) or Service User record; conduct a lessons-learned assessment; address any care issues within normal management arrangements.

Where 'additional information required' is the outcome: agree a plan with the Clinical Lead, signed off by the CEO, specifying the information needed, resources, and immediate safety/protective issues.

Where 'reasonable grounds for concern' is the outcome: develop a Safeguarding Plan in partnership with the Management Team within 3 weeks of screening completion; if Service User has capacity and agrees, engage with them on choice and control measures.
Circulate the Preliminary Screening Report to the CEO for review.
Inform the source of the allegation of the Preliminary Screening findings.

Issue the Preliminary Screening Report and any associated plans to the HSE Safeguarding and Protection Team (Vulnerable Persons) for review and advice; from this point on, actions are agreed with the Safeguarding and Protection Team.
Schedule the Safeguarding Plan Review at agreed intervals - within 6 months of Plan commencement and at minimum 6-monthly thereafter or on case closure.
steps.tf: Within 6 months of Plan commencement; 6-monthly thereafter

SOP-008.title: Complete an Incident Report and Categorise Severity
SOP-008.purpose: To ensure every incident, near miss or potential incident is reported, contained, categorised on the HSE Risk Impact Table, and acted upon within defined timeframes - so that Service Users are kept safe, learning is captured, and Liberty Living meets its reporting obligations to the HSE, HIQA and other external bodies.

steps.action: Staff member who identifies the incident takes immediate containment actions: ensure safety/wellbeing of Service User and others, make the environment safe, assess need for medical attention, monitor and reassure the Service User, keep communicating, gather information, secure evidence.
Verbally report the incident to the Client Services Manager immediately (out-of-hours: to on-call coordinator, with next-working-day review).
steps.role: Staff member -> Client Services Manager / on-call

steps.action: Document a factual recollection of events - chronological, objective, confined to the staff member's involvement, no opinions outside expertise, no hostile or defensive comments.

Record the incident on the electronic incident reporting system (or paper form transferred to electronic system) as soon as possible, no later than 3 working days. Capture: Who was involved; Who is aware; When it happened; Where it happened; What happened; What immediate actions were taken; How (chronology); Why (initi
steps.tf: Same shift; no later than 3 working days

steps.action: Client Services Manager is nominated as Incident Owner. Reviews the incident and determines if immediate staff debrief is required.

Calculate the Risk Rating using QA-13 Appendix 2 Impact Scoring Table (Negligible / Minor / Moderate / Major / Extreme) and categorise: Category 3 (Negligible/Minor); Category 2 (Moderate); Category 1 (Major/Extreme).
For Category 1 or Category 2, automatic notifications are generated to senior management (Director of Quality, Director of Operations, CEO).

For Category 1: inform the SAO (CEO) within 24 hours; notify the HSE within 24 hours; convene the SIMT within 5 days; initiate Systems Analysis Review per HSE Incident Management Framework 2020 (see SOP-009).
steps.tf: Within 24 hours (SAO and HSE); SIMT within 5 days

steps.action: Determine external reporting obligations (CEO): HSE (in writing without delay for all incidents/Category 1/SRE); An Garda Siochana (where danger or criminal offence); Health and Safety Authority (dangerous occurrence or staff injury - IR1 form); HPRA (adverse drug reactions); insurers; HIQA notifiable incidents per Pat
Initiate Open Disclosure within 24-48 hours where the incident affects a Service User (SOP-010).
For Category 2 and applicable Category 3 incidents, conduct a root cause analysis (default target 28 days from incident) - see SOP-009.
Identify SMART corrective and preventive actions; review the Risk Register / Individual Risk Management Plans per HS-31; complete a comprehensive incident record.
Director of Quality reviews and closes out the incident once all stages are complete and external reporting is done.
Trend the incident at the Clinical Governance Committee; embed learning into care plans, training, risk registers.

SOP-009.title: Conduct a Root Cause Analysis (Systems Analysis Review)
SOP-009.purpose: To conduct a structured, independent Systems Analysis Review of every Category 1 incident (Major or Extreme on the HSE Risk Impact Table) - and to apply proportionate root cause analysis to Category 2 (Moderate) and applicable Category 3 incidents - using HSE Incident Management Framework methodology, so that systems failures are understood, recommendations are credible, and learning embeds across Liberty Living.

steps.action: SAO ensures the SIMT is convened within 5 days of incident notification. SIMT membership is kept to a manageable number with access to additional expertise.
SIMT determines whether a Systems Analysis is required. Where it is not required for a Category 1, the decision is supported by reasons and signed off by the SAO.

Where Systems Analysis is required, agree the Terms of Reference: background, purpose, scope, SIMT membership, Review Team membership (independent, with subject-matter expertise and systems-analysis experience), timeframe (target 125 days from incident occurrence), reporting structure, Service User Liaison Person and S

SAO formally engages with the Service User/relevant person at the earliest opportunity: check on wellbeing, advise of the review plan and how to engage, introduce the Designated Liaison Person and provide contact details, explain what to expect.

Client Services Manager advises staff of the review plan, informs them that the Review Team may seek their perspective, introduces the Designated Support Person, and requests written personal recollections.
steps.role: Client Services Manager + Staff Support Person

steps.action: SAO requests collation of all relevant documentation: Service User records, policies and procedures, training records, rosters, equipment maintenance records.

Conduct the analysis using systems and human factors theory. Use Fishbone diagrams, 5 Whys, and the Yorkshire Contributory Factors Framework (HSE Incident Management Framework Section 13). Shift focus to system performance and underlying factors.
Express findings as Statements of Findings - describing relationships between contributing factors and the incident/harm.
Make recommendations linked to the analysis and findings - focused on addressing systems weaknesses, SMART, practical and proportionate.

Draft the report using the HSE Systems Analysis Review Report Template. Quality-assure prior to finalising: report within TOR scope; process consistent with approach; clear linkages between analysis, findings and recommendations; recommendations practical and proportionate; principles of natural justice observed.
SIMT recommends sign-off to SAO; if a SIMT member also sat on the Review Team they absent themselves from sign-off discussions (recorded in minutes). SAO signs off the report.

Designated Liaison Person contacts the Service User/relevant person personally to advise that the report is finalised; arrangements made to provide the report in person where possible, with an opportunity to reiterate the apology and ask about wellbeing/support.
Staff are advised of the outcome supportively (a learning session with slides is recommended) and provided with a copy of the report.
steps.role: Client Services Manager + Staff Support Person

steps.action: HSE is provided with the Investigation Report.

SOP-010.title: Hold an Open Disclosure Initial Meeting and Issue Open Disclosure Letter
SOP-010.purpose: To ensure Liberty Living communicates openly, honestly, compassionately and on time with every Service User (or relevant person) affected by a patient safety incident - including a sincere apology, the facts known, the plan for further investigation, and a written record within statutory timeframes under the Patient Safety (Notifiable Incidents and Open Disclosure) Act 2023 or the Civil Liability (Amendment) Act 2017

steps.action: Initiate open disclosure within 24-48 hours of the service becoming aware of the incident - this window includes preparation and planning for the open disclosure meeting.
Appoint a Designated Liaison Person for the Service User and provide full contact details to the Service User/relevant person.
Appoint the Open Disclosure Team - CEO, Designated Liaison Person, and others relevant to the Service User's care and the incident.

Step 1 Preparation: assess the Service User's circumstances and the nature of the incident; consider written follow-up requirements; consult appropriate persons/staff to establish facts available at the time; determine to whom open disclosure will be made; present information clearly; determine the apology and its word
Inform the Service User/relevant person of the incident and the purpose of the meeting (explaining open disclosure). Agree a venue that ensures privacy and comfort.

Step 2 Open Disclosure Meeting: introduce all present and their roles; explain notes will be taken; invite the Service User/relevant person to talk about their understanding, impact, and expectations; listen and respond.

Offer a sincere and meaningful apology - saying what is known, expressing regret, taking responsibility for what is being investigated. Apologies given in good faith are protected under the Civil Liability (Amendment) Act 2017 for non-notifiable incidents.

Describe the incident: date of the incident where known; the facts available at the time; how the investigation will proceed; what the Service User can expect; details of the Designated Liaison Person and support services (including independent advocacy).

Close the meeting: summarise salient points and agreed actions; check for outstanding concerns; confirm the Designated Liaison Person is the point of contact; signal further open disclosure meeting if needed; advise that a written record will follow.

Step 3 Documentation and Written Follow-up: produce the written record/letter containing: date of meeting; summary of information shared; sincere and meaningful apology; details of the Designated Liaison Person; signed by the CEO. Include in the relevant incident report.
steps.tf: Within 5 calendar days where Patient Safety Act 2023 or Civil Liability (Amendment) Act 20

steps.action: For notifiable incidents under the Patient Safety Act 2023: the written record must state that the open disclosure was made pursuant to and in compliance with Section 5(1) of the Patient Safety (Notifiable Incidents and Open Disclosure) Act 2023. For non-notifiable incidents where Civil Liability (Amendment) Act 2017 p
steps.tf: Within 5 calendar days of the meeting

steps.action: Step 4 Ongoing Communication: keep the Service User/relevant person updated as the investigation progresses; the Designated Liaison Person is the constant point of contact.
Step 5 Post-Disclosure: support for Service User, family and staff after disclosure; offer independent advocacy; agree ongoing care plan adjustments; review learning.
steps.role: Designated Liaison Person + Client Services Manager

SOP-011.title: Receive and Acknowledge a Verbal Complaint
SOP-011.purpose: To make sure every verbal complaint a Liberty Living staff member receives - typically at a care call - is listened to, acknowledged, resolved at point of contact where possible, and logged in the Master Complaints Log within the same shift, so that no concern is lost and acknowledgement is issued within 5 working days where the complaint cannot be resolved locally.

steps.action: Listen to the complainant. Do not interrupt, defend or argue. Reassure them that raising a concern will not adversely affect their care.
Acknowledge the concern and apologise that they have had to raise it (an apology for the experience, not necessarily for fault). Thank them for telling Liberty Living.

Where the matter is within scope and capability, attempt local resolution at the point of contact (e.g. correct an arrangement, explain a misunderstanding, escalate to Supervisor immediately for resolution).

Confirm with the complainant whether they consider the matter resolved at the point of contact. Where the complainant agrees, record the outcome; where the complainant is not satisfied, advise them the matter will be referred to the Client Services Manager / Complaints Officer.

Record the complaint in the Master Complaints Log (QA-SOP-CMP-01) the same shift - capture: Complaint ID; Date Received; Quarter; Cluster/Area; Primary Theme; Severity Level; Safeguarding Concern Y/N; Incident Triggered Y/N; Clinical Review Required Y/N. Forward a Complaint Form copy to the Complaints Officer.
Where the complaint cannot be resolved at point of contact, the Client Services Manager is assigned and attempts resolution.
Where the complaint remains unresolved, escalate to the Complaints Officer who issues a written acknowledgement to the complainant within 5 working days of receipt.
If at any point a safeguarding concern is identified, activate SOP-005 same day; if an incident has occurred, activate SOP-008. Flag both in the Complaints Log.
Complete the auto-calculated Log fields: Resolution Days, Resolved within 30 Days Y/N - once the complaint is closed.

SOP-012.title: Investigate and Respond to a Formal Complaint
SOP-012.purpose: To investigate and respond to every formal complaint that has not been resolved at point of contact - within Health Act 2004 Part 9 timeframes - so that the complainant receives a fair, impartial, evidence-based outcome, learning is embedded, and external escalation rights (Internal Review, Office of the Ombudsman, HSE, HIQA) are clearly communicated.

steps.action: Acknowledge the complaint in writing to the complainant within 5 working days of receipt - confirming who is investigating, indicative timeframe, and the complainant's rights.
Assess the complaint for scope, urgency, and any safeguarding / incident / clinical review flags. Where excluded matters arise, write to the complainant within 5 working days with reasons.
Develop an investigation plan: documents to review, interviewees, expected timeframe. Agree with Director of Quality if complaint is complex.
Carry out the investigation: review CRM, Service User record, home folder, training and supervision records, rosters; interview staff and complainant; gather evidence; check policies and procedures.

Where the 30-working-day timeframe cannot be met, write to the complainant within 30 working days explaining the delay and the new indicative date - and provide updates every 20 working days thereafter.
Produce a findings document with: facts established; analysis against policy; whether the complaint is upheld in full, in part, or not upheld; recommendations (SMART); apology where applicable.

Issue a written response (outcome letter) to the complainant within 30 working days of receipt - containing the findings, apology where applicable, recommendations and the complainant's right to seek an Internal Review within Liberty Living and onward escalation to the Office of the Ombudsman / HSE / HIQA.
Director of Operations implements recommendations; Complaints Officer monitors and confirms implementation.

Where the complainant requests an Internal Review (in writing or email) within 30 working days of the outcome letter, the Complaints Review Officer acknowledges in writing within 5 working days and commences the review within 20 working days.
steps.tf: Acknowledgement within 5 WD; review within 20 WD

steps.action: Complaints Review Officer completes the review within 20 working days and issues findings (extension permitted with written notice and new target date).
steps.tf: Within 20 working days of review request

steps.action: Where the complainant remains unsatisfied, advise of external escalation routes - Office of the Ombudsman (Ombudsman Act 1980 as amended), HSE, HIQA - and provide contact details.
Update the Master Complaints Log with Root Cause, Assigned Manager, Date Closed; auto-calculated fields Resolution Days, Resolved within 30 Days Y/N; Reported to HSE Y/N; QIP Raised Y/N.
