Circulate learnings to staff; trend complaint themes quarterly to HSE and annually for Clinical Governance Committee.

SOP-013.title: Submit a Garda National Vetting Bureau Application (eVetting)
SOP-013.purpose: To ensure every Liberty Living staff member is Garda-vetted before commencing work with vulnerable adults, in line with the National Vetting Bureau (Children and Vulnerable Persons) Acts 2012-2016, and to manage re-vetting and any change to vetting status during employment.

steps.action: Issue a Vetting Invitation Form to the candidate, requiring them to provide proof of identity totalling 100 points (with at least one photographic ID) per An Garda Siochana 100-point check system.
Validate the candidate's proof of identity against the 100-point check before any vetting submission. Where ID is insufficient, request additional documents.

Send the candidate an email with the eVetting link inviting them to complete the Vetting Application Form online. The candidate must disclose all convictions - including driving offences, non-payment of TV licence, public order offences, and matters resulting in probation or community service (Republic of Ireland and N
steps.tf: Within 5 working days of identity validation

steps.action: Candidate completes and signs the online Vetting Application Form, authorising the Garda Vetting process, and submits to the HR Director.
HR Director (Liaison Person) reviews the Vetting Application Form, confirms it is complete and accurate, and submits it to the National Vetting Bureau via eVetting.
steps.tf: Within 2 working days of candidate submission

steps.action: Track the application. Where the candidate has lived outside Ireland (or in Northern Ireland) for 6 months or more after age 18, request an Overseas Police Clearance Certificate (original received and filed; copy returned to applicant).
Receive the Vetting Disclosure directly from the NVB. Review it against the candidate's declarations.
Provide a copy of the Disclosure to the vetting subject (candidate) as soon as practicable.

Where the vetting process identifies undeclared convictions/Specified Information, or additional convictions beyond those declared, the candidate may be permanently removed from the selection process.

Where convictions or Specified Information have been declared and confirmed, the CEO and HR Director carry out a documented risk assessment: severity, candidate's explanation, recency, age at offence, current age, pattern vs one-off, contributing circumstances, relevance to the role. Decision documented.
Communicate the outcome to the candidate. Where unsuccessful, candidate retains right to challenge through National Vetting Bureau procedures.
Store the Vetting Disclosure and all correspondence securely with restricted access; retain only as long as necessary per IM-05 (highly sensitive personal data under GDPR / Data Protection Act 2018).
Schedule re-vetting for the staff member during employment in line with Liberty Living's vetting cycle; manage re-vetting using the same procedure.

Where a staff member notifies a change to their criminal record or Specified Information status during employment, escalate to the CEO immediately; this may trigger re-vetting and, where necessary, suspension/removal from roster pending assessment.

SOP-014.title: Run a Staff Supervision Session
SOP-014.purpose: To provide planned, protected, confidential one-to-one supervision for every Home Support Worker / HCA - at minimum quarterly - so that performance is monitored, competency gaps are addressed, learning needs are identified, and Service User safety and quality are protected.

steps.action: For new HCAs: deliver one-to-one supervision for the first 8 hours of their assignments, ideally covering more than one Service User. Reassessment of competency runs alongside.
Schedule formal supervision at least quarterly - adjust frequency upwards based on staff experience, Service User complexity, and training needs. Inform the staff member in advance.

Hold the session in private; ensure it is planned, protected and uninterrupted. Confirm confidentiality, with the explicit exception that any disclosure of malpractice, misconduct, negligence or risk to Service User wellbeing will be acted upon under HS-23.1 and QA-13.
Open with progress since last supervision: review previous actions, training completed, Service Users supported.
Discuss good working practices and praise where appropriate; identify problems and solutions; explore work-related issues impacting practice.
Discuss personal and professional development wishes, career goals, and supports needed.
Agree SMART actions for the next supervision cycle (training, shadowing, observation, additional support). Set the next supervision date.
Sign and date the Supervision Record (both Supervisor and staff member). Store securely in the staff file; access restricted.

Where competency gaps or performance concerns are identified, escalate to the Clinical Lead / Client Services Manager; agree additional supervision requirements. Where the concern indicates risk to a Service User, manage under QA-13 incident process.
steps.role: Supervisor -> Client Services Manager / Clinical Lead

steps.action: Where the supervision reveals matters of malpractice, misconduct, negligence or risk to others, act under HS-23.1 (safeguarding) and/or QA-37 (protected disclosures). Confidentiality of supervision does not protect such disclosures.
Conduct annual appraisal completed by Supervisor / Client Services Manager; outcomes stored in the staff record and fed into training plan and competency reassessment (annual NCCA).
HR Director / Supervisors carry out spot checks and audits to confirm supervision compliance across the workforce.

SOP-015.title: Respond to a Suspected Personal Data Breach
SOP-015.purpose: To ensure every suspected or confirmed personal data breach is contained, assessed, internally escalated, and (where required) notified to the Data Protection Commission within 72 hours and to affected Data Subjects without undue delay where there is a high risk to their rights and freedoms - meeting Liberty Living's obligations under GDPR / Data Protection Act 2018.

steps.action: Any staff member identifying a possible personal data breach reports it immediately to the Data Protection Officer (dpo@libertyhomecare.ie) - by phone if urgent. Do not delay to gather full information.
Take immediate containment actions: stop further disclosure (e.g. recall the email, secure the device, change passwords, isolate the affected system). Preserve evidence for investigation.

DPO, in conjunction with the Data Controller (CEO via Director of Operations), opens a breach investigation to quickly establish whether a personal data breach has occurred. Engage IT Support Services or external Data Processors as required.

Establish the likelihood and severity of the resulting risk to the Data Subject's rights and freedoms - categories of data, number of records, number of individuals affected, special category data Y/N, financial data Y/N, mitigating factors (e.g. encryption).

Director of Care informs the Key Contact Person at the HSE within 24 hours of any actual or suspected breach giving rise to actual or potential loss, theft, unauthorised release or disclosure of information - with all relevant facts surrounding the incident.

Determine whether the DPC must be notified. The DPC MUST be notified unless the breach is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of Data Subjects (e.g. lost laptop with strong encryption). Liberty Living is exempt only where the full extent has been reported directly to the affected Data Subject(s), no

Where the DPC must be notified, do so without undue delay and no later than 72 hours after becoming aware of the breach. Use the DPC 'Report a breach' online form (https://forms.dataprotection.ie/report-a-breach-of-personal-data) - preferred - or email (no personal data in initial contact).

Notification to the DPC must include: nature of the breach (categories and approximate number of individuals; categories and approximate number of records); DPO contact details; likely consequences; measures taken/proposed to deal with the breach and mitigate adverse effects.
Where the full investigation cannot be completed within 72 hours, the DPC accepts the required information in phases - provided this is done without further undue delay.

Notify affected Data Subjects promptly where there is a HIGH risk that their personal data has been obtained or used inappropriately. Notification (clear, plain language) must include: nature of the breach; DPO contact details; likely consequences; measures taken/proposed to deal with the breach and mitigate. The DPO m
Where appropriate, notify other organisations that may help protect Data Subjects (e.g. An Garda Siochana for criminal activity, financial institutions for financial data) per IM-05 Section 12.12.

Take all appropriate actions to contain the breach, assess and address potential adverse consequences for individuals, and address the root cause to prevent recurrence - coordinated by the DPO with the Data Controller.

Record the breach in the Breach Register maintained under IM-05 - including the facts, effects, remedial action - irrespective of whether it was notifiable. The breach must be documented even where no notification is required (Article 33(5) GDPR).
Raise a QA-13 incident report for any breach causing harm or distress to Service Users; consider Open Disclosure under SOP-010 where appropriate.

SOP-016.title: Conduct an Annual Care Plan Review
SOP-016.purpose: To set out how Liberty Living conducts the annual comprehensive review of each Service User's Home Support Care Plan so that the plan remains responsive to their evolving needs, preferences, health status and risks, in line with CARE-08 Section 10.

steps.action: Schedule the annual review visit at least 4 weeks in advance. Confirm date, time and attendees (Service User, family/representative per Final Consent for Sharing) and book in the CRM diary.

Prepare the review pack: HSE referral documentation, latest correspondence, daily diary notes, incident reports, complaints, HCA observations, current Care Plan, all three risk assessments (Environmental, Falls, Medication) and the Risk Management Plan. Print blank Home Care Record Sheets and the Client Review Liberty

Conduct the review in the Service User's home using the Client Review Liberty HomeCare form. Cover: Service User satisfaction, health and functional status, medication risks, environmental risks, social changes, consent status, HCA observations, and any changes since last review.

Re-administer the three core risk assessments (Environmental, Falls Risk, Medication Risk) and update the Risk Management Plan to reflect any positive or negative change in risk. Document any new control measures.
Re-confirm Service User consent for care interventions and information sharing per CARE-06. Confirm or update the Final Consent for Sharing form and Consent to Prompt Medication.

Update the Final Support and Care Plan to reflect findings: revised SMART goals, schedule of services, personal care routines, medication support, mobility and transfer needs, communication needs, social and cultural considerations, and new review date. Reflect preferences including preferred gender of HCA for personal
Submit the updated Care Plan to the Clinical Lead for review and approval, ensuring alignment with HSE referral requirements for HSE-funded clients.

Where changes to the level or type of support are required for HSE-funded Service Users, submit the proposed alteration with Clinical Lead sign-off to the HSE Home Support Resource Manager. Do not implement any change to HSE-funded support without HSE approval.

Communicate the review outcome and agreed changes in writing to the Service User and representative within 10 working days of determination, and confirm next review date (no later than 12 months hence, or sooner if triggered).
Communicate the updated Care Plan and any changes in tasks/routines to assigned HCA(s) via the CRM mobile app and a verbal briefing. Confirm HCA acknowledgement before next visit.

File the completed Client Review form, updated risk assessments, updated Care Plan and HSE correspondence in the Service User's physical and electronic file. Place active Care Plan and Schedule in the Service User's home folder.

SOP-017.title: Deliver a Routine Care Visit
SOP-017.purpose: To set out the step-by-step process every Liberty Living HCA shall follow when delivering a routine scheduled home support visit so that care is safe, person-centred, dignified, and delivered in full accordance with the authorised Home Support Care Plan.

steps.action: Before leaving for the visit, review the Service User's Home Support Care Plan and Schedule of Services in the Careplanner app. Note any updates, new risks, gender preferences for personal care, infection precautions, and prescribed tasks for the call.
Arrive at the Service User's home at the scheduled time. Clock in by scanning the NFC tag using the Careplanner app to record arrival on the LibertyLiving_RecordSheet timeline.
Introduce yourself by name and role. Show photo ID. Confirm Service User identity. Use the password protection process if first contact. Greet by preferred name.
Perform hand hygiene per HS-34 (WHO Five Moments). Don appropriate PPE based on the Point-of-Care Risk Assessment (PCRA Laminated card) and the Service User's infection status.
Confirm ongoing consent for each care task. Where the Service User refuses any element, respect the refusal under CARE-02, document immediately and notify the office before leaving where safe.

Deliver each authorised task per the Care Plan: personal and intimate care per CARE-33 (respecting dignity, privacy, gender preference); medication prompting per CARE-20 (We Prompt. We Do NOT Administer.); mobility using safe manual handling; nutrition/hydration; environmental tasks. Do not deviate from the Care Plan.

Throughout the visit, observe and report any change in the Service User's condition, environment, mood, skin integrity, mobility, appetite or behaviour. Note any safeguarding indicators (HS-23 and HS-23.1).

Complete the LibertyLiving_RecordSheet_2026_v1 in the home folder: arrival/departure times, tasks completed, medication prompted (with time), food/fluid intake where applicable, observations and your signature.

Perform final hand hygiene, dispose of PPE per HS-15, clean and return equipment used, and ensure the environment is left safe (electric points, hot taps, stove, locked doors per Service User's wishes).
Clock out via the Careplanner NFC tag on departure. Submit the CRM diary note within the call covering what was delivered, observations, refusals and any issues to escalate.

Escalate concerns, refusals, deterioration, environmental risks or safeguarding indicators to the on-call Supervisor or Client Services Manager (Mary James / John Mathias / Alison Carroll / Tina Zambra / Kelsie Leavy) without delay. Out of hours, use the on-call rota.
steps.tf: Same day; immediately for safety issues

SOP-018.title: Respond to a Missed or Cancelled Visit
SOP-018.purpose: To set out the standard response when a scheduled Home Support visit is missed, cancelled or shortened, so that Service User safety is preserved, the cause is documented, HSE reporting requirements are met and an incident is opened where required under QA-13.

steps.action: On detection of a missed/late/cancelled visit, confirm the facts: which Service User, scheduled time, scheduled HCA, the priority rating (P1/P2/P3), and the reason known so far.

Phone the Service User immediately to confirm welfare and explain. If no answer, attempt next-of-kin. If still no contact and Service User is P1/P2 or lives alone, escalate immediately to a welfare check by another HCA, family or An Garda Síochána as appropriate.

Identify and dispatch a suitably qualified replacement HCA to cover the visit where possible. Confirm the replacement has reviewed the Care Plan and notify the Service User of the substitution and revised arrival time.

Determine the cause and category: HCA absence, Service User cancellation (with/without 8 hours notice), refusal, no-access, severe weather, system failure, or shortened call. Apply GOV-25 rules: cancellations with <8 hours notice attract 50% HSE rate; shortened calls require HSE notification.
Where the visit could not be delivered, open a Final Incident Report (QA-13) categorising severity. Missed visits to high-dependency (P1) Service Users default to Category 2 minimum.

Where the Service User is HSE-funded, notify the HSE Home Support Resource Manager of the missed/shortened/cancelled call without delay, capturing reason and corrective action (GOV-25 Section 9.7, 10.3-10.4).
steps.role: Director of Operations / Client Services Manager
steps.tf: Same working day for serious; next working day otherwise

steps.action: Where the missed visit caused or could have caused harm, initiate Open Disclosure within 24-48 hours per QA-13 Section 9.0. Apologise, explain the facts, set out actions being taken.
steps.role: Director of Quality / Director of Operations

steps.action: Conduct a root cause review (5 whys for Category 2/3). Identify systemic causes — rostering, communication, geography, HCA capacity, weather — and assign SMART corrective actions with owners and target dates.
steps.role: Director of Quality / Client Services Manager

steps.action: Update the Schedule of Services and capture the missed call in the monthly HSE invoice statement of missed calls and reasons per GOV-25 Section 6.12.6 and CARE-08 Section 10.13.
steps.role: Director of Finance / Client Services Manager

steps.action: Where repeated missed visits emerge for the same Service User or HCA, flag for Executive Management Team review and consider revisions to staffing, allocation, training or the Care Plan.
steps.role: Director of Quality / Director of Operations

SOP-019.title: Respond to a Medication Error or Near Miss
SOP-019.purpose: To establish the immediate, safety-led response Liberty Living shall take when a medication error or near miss occurs in connection with a medication prompt — and to reinforce the framing principle: We Prompt. We Do NOT Administer.

steps.action: ENSURE IMMEDIATE SAFETY. If the Service User shows signs of an Adverse Drug Reaction (rash, wheezing, swelling, difficulty breathing, dizziness, nausea, loss of consciousness), call 112/999 without delay. Provide Basic Life Support if trained. Stay with the Service User and follow the call handler's instructions.

Remember the framing principle: We Prompt. We Do NOT Administer. Do not attempt to retrieve, give an antidote, induce vomiting, or take any clinical decision. The HCA's role is limited to prompting per CARE-20 Section 7.0.

Notify the on-call Supervisor / Client Services Manager immediately by phone. Provide: Service User name, what happened, time, medication details, current condition, and whether emergency services have been called.

Supervisor/CSM contacts the Service User's representative (per Final Consent for Sharing) and the GP immediately. The CSM informs the Clinical Lead (Ana Lyons) and Director of Quality (Lisa McDonagh, 085 800 3961).

HCA completes a contemporaneous narrative entry in the Service User's home record and CRM diary: chronological factual account — medication name (as labelled), dose, time, Service User's reaction (signs/symptoms), and immediate actions taken. Objective, factual; no interpretation.
steps.tf: Same working day; ideally before leaving the home

steps.action: Open a Final Incident Report (QA-13 Appendix 6) within 3 working days. Categorise severity: Category 1 (Major/Extreme), Category 2 (Moderate), or Category 3 (Minor/Negligible — near miss/no harm). Medication errors involving harm default to Category 1 or 2.

For Category 1, Executive Management Team notifies the Senior Accountable Officer (CEO Shauna Delaney) within 24 hours; the HSE is notified within 24 hours; the SIMT is convened within 5 days; a Systems Analysis review is initiated per QA-13 Section 8.0.

Initiate the Open Disclosure process within 24-48 hours per QA-13 Section 9.0. The CEO leads, appointing a Designated Liaison Person. A sincere and meaningful apology is offered; written follow-up issued within 5 calendar days (Civil Liability (Amendment) Act 2017 Part 4; Patient Safety Act 2023 for notifiable incident

Where the error involves a notifiable incident under the Patient Safety (Notifiable Incidents and Open Disclosure) Act 2023, follow SOP-026 to make the statutory notification within the statutory window.

Conduct root cause analysis (5 whys / fishbone) for Category 1/2, focusing on systemic factors — Care Plan clarity, labelling, training, consent process, handover. Identify SMART corrective actions and assign owners and target dates.
steps.tf: Within 125 days for Category 1; 28 days for Category 2

steps.action: Provide structured staff support to the HCA per QA-13 Section 12.0. Assign a Staff Support Person; do not discipline a staff member who has reported promptly and in good faith (QA-13 Section 6.3). Distinguish system failure from individual misconduct.
steps.role: Client Services Manager / Director of HR

SOP-020.title: Respond to a Deterioration in Service User Condition
SOP-020.purpose: To establish a clear, time-bound response when an HCA or other staff member identifies a deterioration in a Service User's physical, cognitive or psychological condition, so that escalation is timely, clinical advice is sought, the Care Plan is reviewed, and where required an incident and reassessment are opened.

steps.action: On observing signs of deterioration, ensure the immediate safety of the Service User. Stay with them and offer reassurance. Where there is any airway/breathing/circulation compromise, suspected stroke (FAST), suspected sepsis, chest pain, severe bleeding, or loss of consciousness, call 112/999 immediately.

Do not undertake a clinical assessment (HCAs are non-clinical). Note objective observations: time, what was observed (rate of breathing if visible, colour, responsiveness, behaviour), and the Service User's verbal report.
Phone the on-call Supervisor / CSM immediately. Provide: Service User name and address; observations; whether 112/999 has been called; family contact attempted; current location and stability.
steps.tf: Within 5 minutes of identification (after step 1 if emergency)

steps.action: Contact the Service User's representative (per Final Consent for Sharing) and the GP/Public Health Nurse to advise of deterioration and seek clinical guidance. Out of hours, contact the GP out-of-hours service (CareDoc, NowDoc, SouthDoc).

Stay with the Service User until paramedics arrive (if 112/999 called), or until family/clinician takes over. Follow paramedic/clinician instructions. Do not leave the Service User alone if concerned.
Where the Service User is transported to hospital, ensure the home folder, current medication list and Care Plan accompany them. Notify the office and document hospital and ward.

Open a Final Incident Report (QA-13) categorising severity. Deterioration leading to hospital admission is at minimum Category 2; deterioration leading to major harm or death is Category 1 and triggers the SIMT and HSE notification within 24 hours.
steps.tf: Within 3 working days (24 hours for Category 1)

steps.action: Escalate clinically to the Clinical Lead (Ana Lyons, 085 723 4679) for professional oversight. The Clinical Lead reviews following any escalation of clinical risk or deterioration (CARE-08 Section 5.3).

Update the Home Support Care Plan to reflect new clinical needs, equipment, support hours or PPE requirements. Where additional HSE-funded hours are recommended, submit to HSE Home Support Resource Manager (no implementation without HSE approval).

Initiate Open Disclosure where the deterioration was associated with a service failure, missed call, or where Liberty Living's actions/omissions contributed (QA-13 Section 9.0). Communicate within 24-48 hours; apologise sincerely.
Communicate the updated Care Plan to the HCA team via CRM and verbal handover before the next visit. Confirm receipt and understanding from each assigned HCA.

SOP-021.title: Respond to Suspected Infection
SOP-021.purpose: To set out the action Liberty Living staff shall take when an infection is suspected in a Service User (or in themselves), so that transmission is prevented, the Service User receives timely clinical attention, and Transmission-based Precautions are implemented per HS-15 in addition to Standard Precautions and Hand Hygiene HS-34.

steps.action: Perform a Point-of-Care Risk Assessment (PCRA) using the PCRA Laminated card BEFORE entering the home and before each care task. Identify likelihood of exposure and appropriate PPE.
steps.tf: Before entering home and before each task

steps.action: Apply Standard Precautions at all times: WHO Five Moments hand hygiene per HS-34, appropriate PPE, respiratory hygiene/cough etiquette, waste segregation, safe laundry, environmental cleaning. These apply whether infection is known or not (HIQA, 2018).

On observation of signs/symptoms (fever, cough, diarrhoea, vomiting, rash, wound discharge, confusion suggestive of sepsis), record observations objectively in the Service User record. Do not undertake clinical diagnosis — escalate.

Implement Transmission-based Precautions appropriate to suspected route (Contact, Droplet, Airborne — HS-15 Section 10.0). Examples: gloves and apron for contact; FFP2/surgical mask for droplet; FFP2 + eye protection for airborne (measles, suspected TB).

Phone the Supervisor / CSM immediately to report the suspected infection. Provide observations, time of onset (where known), Service User stability, whether emergency intervention is needed (signs of sepsis = 999).
steps.tf: Same visit, before leaving where possible

steps.action: CSM contacts the Service User's GP/Public Health Nurse for clinical assessment. Where a notifiable infectious disease is suspected (measles, TB, COVID-19, scabies, MRSA), follow HSE Public Health guidance and seek advice from a Community Infection Prevention and Control Nurse (CIPCN).

Where confirmed colonisation/infection status is communicated (hospital discharge, GP, PHN), update the Care Plan to document the infection, Transmission-based Precautions required, PPE needed and duration. Communicate to all assigned HCAs before next visit.

Where suspected infection is an outbreak (2+ Service Users with similar symptoms linked by place/time), notify the Director of Quality (Lisa McDonagh) immediately. The CEO/Director of Operations notifies HIQA, HSE and Department of Public Health where disease is notifiable (HS-15 Section 5.3).
steps.role: Director of Operations / Director of Quality

steps.action: Where the HCA is the suspected source or has been exposed (needlestick, contact with bodily fluids), the HCA must not attend further work shifts. Refer to Occupational Health and follow HS-15 management of sharps and exposure protocols.
Open an incident in the Final Incident Report system where harm or risk of harm occurred — particularly hospital admission, outbreak, or staff exposure. Categorise per QA-13.
steps.role: Client Services Manager / Director of Quality

steps.action: Conduct contact tracing where required — identify all HCAs and Service Users potentially exposed during the infectious period. Coordinate with Public Health on testing, quarantine and communication.

SOP-022.title: Submit a HSE Safeguarding & Protection Team Notification
SOP-022.purpose: To set out how Liberty Living submits a safeguarding concern or allegation of abuse to the HSE Safeguarding and Protection Team (Vulnerable Persons) within three working days, in accordance with HS-23.1 and the HSE National Policy and Procedures for Safeguarding Vulnerable Persons at Risk of Abuse (2014).

steps.action: On receipt of the concern, log it immediately in the Safeguarding Log (the HS-23 register) and timestamp. Assign a unique safeguarding reference. Ensure the Incident Report Form completed by the staff member is filed with the case.
Inform the CEO Shauna Delaney without delay so the CEO can discharge their accountability for safeguarding response (HS-23.1 Section 5.2).

Conduct an immediate risk assessment to determine ongoing risk to the vulnerable person and put in place any immediate protective measures — e.g. removing the alleged perpetrator from the Service User's care, additional welfare checks, or contacting An Garda Síochána where a criminal offence may have occurred.

Where there is reason to believe a criminal offence may have been committed or is about to be committed, contact An Garda Síochána immediately. Do not interfere with potential evidence — preserve scene, do not clean, do not wash clothing, do not handle objects (HS-23.1 Section 9.1.6).
steps.role: Designated Safeguarding Officer / Client Services Manager

steps.action: Initiate the Preliminary Screening Process per HS-23.1 Section 9.2 in conjunction with the Clinical Lead. Use HSE Preliminary Screening Form (PSF1). The screening must take account of all relevant readily available information to determine whether an abusive act could have occurred and whether reasonable grounds for co
steps.tf: Commenced same day; completed within 3 working days

steps.action: Notify the HSE Safeguarding and Protection Team (Vulnerable Persons) of the concern within three working days of the DSO becoming aware (or immediately if urgent) per HS-23.1 Section 9.1.12. Send the Preliminary Screening Report and any associated plans to the local Safeguarding and Protection Team.

Notify the HSE nominated point of contact (Managing Director of Public Health Nursing / Complex Care Coordinator) immediately the allegation has been made per HS-23.1 Section 9.1.12 — this is a separate notification to the Safeguarding Team.
steps.tf: As soon as possible after allegation made

steps.action: Contact the vulnerable person's family/representative per HS-23.1 Section 9.1.14, taking account of the Service User's wishes and capacity to consent to information sharing. Where a Decision Supporter is appointed, inform them immediately.
Where applicable, commence Open Disclosure with the Service User and/or representative within 24-48 hours per HS-23.1 Section 9.1.13 and QA-13.

Complete the Preliminary Screening Outcome Sheet (PSF2) recording the views and wishes of the affected individual, precise nature of the allegation, screening findings, and any recommendations. Circulate to the CEO for review.

Where reasonable grounds for concern exist, develop a Safeguarding Plan with the Service User (where they have capacity and consent) per HS-23.1 Section 9.3. The Plan shall be formulated by the DSO in partnership with the Management Team.
steps.tf: Within 5 working days of PSF outcome

steps.action: Maintain ongoing communication with the HSE Safeguarding and Protection Team. All subsequent actions must be in agreement with the Team. Track implementation of the Safeguarding Plan and provide reports at agreed review intervals per HS-23.1 Section 9.2.11.

SOP-023.title: Submit a Tusla Referral (Child Protection Concern)
SOP-023.purpose: To set out how Liberty Living submits a child protection or welfare referral to Tusla — the Child and Family Agency — in accordance with HS-28, the Children First Act 2015 and Children First: National Guidance for the Protection and Welfare of Children (2017), and to meet Mandated Persons' statutory reporting obligations.

steps.action: On becoming aware of a concern, ensure the immediate physical safety of the child. Under no circumstances is the child to be left in a situation of exposure to harm or risk of harm pending Tusla intervention. Where there is immediate or serious risk, contact An Garda Síochána who can bring the child to a place of safet

Where the child discloses abuse, respond with sensitivity and professionalism per HS-28 Appendix 3 'Responding to a Child's Disclosure of Abuse'. Listen calmly, do not promise confidentiality, do not interrogate, reassure the child, and explain what will happen next.

Bring the concern to the attention of the Designated Liaison Person (DLP) immediately — Lisa McDonagh, Director of Operations. If DLP unavailable, contact Deputy DLP Ana Lyons, Clinical Lead (085 723 4679 / anastasia@libertyhomecare.ie). The DLP is contactable 24/7.

DLP reports the matter to the relevant Mandated Person (Clinical Lead). The Mandated Person has the statutory obligation under the Children First Act 2015 to report mandated concerns to Tusla; this obligation cannot be discharged by the DLP on their behalf, but a joint report can be made (HS-28 Section 5.0 — Mandated P

Mandated Person and/or DLP review whether the concern meets 'reasonable grounds for concern' per HS-28 Section 8.5: specific indication by the child of abuse, account by witness, evidence of injury consistent with abuse, behaviour consistent with abuse, or consistent indication of emotional/physical neglect. Where unce

Where applicable, contact the child's parent/guardian to explain the report being made — UNLESS doing so would place the child at further risk of harm. Where parents object to disclosure, document their refusal and explain that the information must be shared for child protection. Document all decisions about parental c

Complete the Tusla Child Protection and Welfare Report Form (CPWRF) — accessible at https://www.tusla.ie/uploads/content/Child_Protection_and_Welfare_Report_Form_FINAL.pdf. Include: child's name, age, address; parents/guardians' names and addresses; name of alleged perpetrator (if known); detailed account of grounds fo
steps.tf: As soon as practicable after concern arises

steps.action: Submit the CPWRF via the Tusla Portal (https://portal.tusla.ie). Where a verbal report is made first (urgent situations), a written report must follow as soon as practicable and no later than 3 days (HS-28 Section 8.6).
steps.tf: Same working day; written report within 3 days if verbal first

steps.action: Where the Mandated Person submits, they must also send a copy of the report to the DLP, or make the report jointly with the DLP. Joint reporting is permitted under the Act (HS-28 Section 5.0).

Consider obligations under the Criminal Justice (Withholding of Information on Offences against Children and Vulnerable Persons) Act 2012. Where information indicating that an offence against a child has been committed has been disclosed, that information must be reported to An Garda Síochána as soon as practicable. Fa

Where the alleged perpetrator is a Liberty Living staff member, manage in parallel under HS-23.1 Responding to Allegations of Abuse Policy and the Trust in Care framework. Suspension may be required pending investigation. Notify the National Vetting Bureau where vetting status is affected.

Cooperate fully with Tusla. The DLP shall work in conjunction with Tusla to support any requested investigation, act on advice received, and provide further information. Monitor the situation and update Tusla as relevant (HS-28 Sections 8.7-8.8).

SOP-024.title: Investigate Suspected Financial Abuse
SOP-024.purpose: To set out how Liberty Living responds to and investigates a suspected financial abuse concern in relation to a Service User, reinforcing the zero gifts policy and the two-signatory rule for high-value transactions, and ensuring escalation under HS-23.1 and CARE-09.

steps.action: On suspecting financial abuse, the HCA reports immediately to their Line Manager / Client Services Manager. Where the staff member identifies large amounts of cash inappropriately stored, this is reported to the CSM same day per CARE-17 Section 8.7. Do not investigate independently.
steps.tf: Same working day; immediately for theft

steps.action: CSM escalates the concern to the Designated Safeguarding Officer Leah O’Brien (085 117 9884 / safeguarding@libertyhomecare.ie) per CARE-09 Section 5.2. DSO informs the Director of Care and CEO.

DSO conducts an immediate risk assessment to determine ongoing risk and put in place protective measures — e.g. suspending the involved staff member's access to the Service User's home; arranging additional welfare checks; safeguarding any remaining funds.

Where there is reason to suspect theft, fraud or other criminal offence, contact An Garda Síochána without delay and preserve any evidence (receipts, bank statements, the Financial Transactions Template, CRM transaction history). Do not interfere with potential evidence per HS-23.1 Section 9.1.6.
steps.role: Designated Safeguarding Officer / Director of Care

steps.action: REINFORCE THE ZERO GIFTS POLICY. Confirm that no staff member has received gifts, money, gratuities or hospitality from the Service User or representatives that may have compromised judgement (CARE-09 Section 6.3; CARE-17 Section 7.1). Any gift previously disclosed to a line manager shall be reviewed in the context of

Initiate the Preliminary Screening Process per HS-23.1 Section 9.2 (financial abuse is a category of abuse under HS-23). Use HSE PSF1 form. The DSO works with the Clinical Lead to establish whether reasonable grounds for concern exist.

Audit the Financial Transactions Template for the Service User: review every transaction in the past 12 months. Reconcile money issued, items purchased, receipts retained, change returned. Apply the TWO-SIGNATORY RULE for transactions exceeding €50 — every such transaction must bear the manager signature per CARE-17 Se
steps.role: Director of Finance / Client Services Manager

steps.action: Notify the HSE Safeguarding and Protection Team (Vulnerable Persons) within three working days of the DSO becoming aware (per SOP-022). Issue the PSF1 and PSF2 forms; agree subsequent actions with the Team.

Where the alleged perpetrator is a Liberty Living staff member, activate the Trust in Care response under HS-23.1 Section 10.0. Suspend the staff member pending investigation under HR-19. Notify the National Vetting Bureau where the disclosure affects vetting status.

Open Disclosure with the Service User and/or representative within 24-48 hours per QA-13 Section 9.0. The CEO leads, providing a sincere apology, factual account, and assurance of investigation and remediation. Issue written follow-up within 5 calendar days (Civil Liability (Amendment) Act 2017 Part 4).

Where reasonable grounds for concern exist, develop a Safeguarding Plan with the Service User (if capacity and consent) per HS-23.1 Section 9.3. The Plan addresses immediate protective measures, support for restitution where loss has occurred, and ongoing monitoring of financial transactions.
steps.tf: Within 5 working days of PSF outcome

steps.action: Update the Service User's Care Plan to reflect tightened controls — dual verification on all transactions, increased audit frequency, restricted staff access to financial activities, additional family/representative oversight. The Director of Care ensures quarterly audits continue with heightened scrutiny.
steps.role: Director of Care / Client Services Manager

steps.action: Where financial loss has occurred and a staff member is found responsible, Liberty Living shall pursue restitution to the Service User and refer to An Garda Síochána. Consider notifying the Service User's insurer where applicable. Notify the HSE under GOV-36.

SOP-025.title: Hold an Open Disclosure Formal Meeting
SOP-025.purpose: To set out how Liberty Living conducts a Formal Open Disclosure meeting following a patient safety incident or notifiable incident, applying the statutory wording requirements under the Patient Safety (Notifiable Incidents and Open Disclosure) Act 2023 and Part 4 of the Civil Liability (Amendment) Act 2017 so that legal protections of the apology are preserved.

steps.action: PREPARATION (QA-13 Step 1). CEO assesses the Service User's/relevant person's circumstances and the nature of the incident. CEO considers requirements including the need for written follow-up. CEO consults with appropriate staff to establish the facts available at the time. CEO determines who will receive the disclosur
steps.tf: Within 24-48 hours of the service becoming aware of the incident

steps.action: Determine the apology required and its wording per QA-13 Section 9.7. An apology must contain: (a) acknowledgement of what has happened and the impact on the Service User/relevant person; (b) explanation of how or why where known; (c) the words 'I am sorry' conveying personal approach and genuine compassion; (d) reassu

Take the Service User's/relevant person's preferences into account when organising the meeting — venue, privacy, comfort, who they wish to bring. Provide full contact details of the Designated Liaison Person.

OPEN DISCLOSURE MEETING (QA-13 Step 2). Make introductions (names and roles). Explain that notes will be taken. Give the Service User/relevant person the opportunity to share their understanding of what happened, the impact, and their expectations. The Open Disclosure Team must listen and respond appropriately.
steps.role: Chief Executive Officer / Open Disclosure Team

steps.action: Deliver the sincere and meaningful apology, using the prepared wording. Recognise the legal protections in place: the apology shall not constitute an admission of fault or liability and shall not be admissible as evidence of fault, professional misconduct or unfitness to practice (Civil Liability (Amendment) Act 2017;

Describe the incident including date, when and how the CEO became aware, facts available at the time, any harm identified (physical or psychological), and any reasonable grounds to believe future harm may develop. Inform the Service User of the service the organisation is providing or proposes to provide to address con

Outline the actions the CEO has taken or proposes to take, and procedures or processes to be implemented, to address learning from the incident. Confirm review or systems analysis underway where applicable (QA-13 Section 8.0).

CLOSE OF MEETING. Summarise salient points and agreed actions. Check with the Service User/relevant person if they have outstanding concerns or questions. Confirm DLP contact. Signal if a further open disclosure meeting will be needed. Provide details of relevant support services (advocacy). Advise that a written recor

DOCUMENTATION & WRITTEN FOLLOW-UP (QA-13 Step 3). Within 5 calendar days for notifiable incidents (Patient Safety Act 2023 / Civil Liability (Amendment) Act 2017 Part 4), share a written record with the Service User/relevant person containing: date of the meeting; summary information provided; sincere and meaningfu
steps.role: Chief Executive Officer / Designated Liaison Person
steps.tf: Within 5 calendar days for notifiable incidents

steps.action: INCLUDE STATUTORY WORDING. Where the incident is a notifiable incident under the Patient Safety (Notifiable Incidents and Open Disclosure) Act 2023, the written record MUST state that the open disclosure was made pursuant to and in compliance with section 5(1) of the Patient Safety (Notifiable Incidents and Open Disclo

ONGOING COMMUNICATION (QA-13 Step 4). Continue communication after the meeting via the Designated Liaison Person. Follow up agreed actions. Schedule additional meetings where new information emerges during incident review. Document all clarification requests and responses; clarifications shall be sought from the CEO.

SHARE REVIEW FINDINGS (QA-13 Step 5). On completion of the incident review (Systems Analysis for Category 1 within 125 days), the Designated Liaison Person arranges to share the findings with the Service User/relevant person in person where possible, providing a copy of the report. Reiterate the apology and inquire abo
steps.tf: On completion of review (within 125 days for Category 1)

steps.action: Seek feedback from the Service User/relevant person and staff involved on the open disclosure process and incorporate any learning. Where a satisfactory conclusion cannot be reached, advise the Service User of alternative courses of action, including the internal complaints process (QA-03).

SOP-026.title: Submit a Patient Safety Act 2023 Statutory Notification
SOP-026.purpose: To set out how Liberty Living submits a statutory notification of a notifiable incident under the Patient Safety (Notifiable Incidents and Open Disclosure) Act 2023, identifying the trigger criteria, the responsible notifier, the statutory window for notification, and the documentation required.

steps.action: IDENTIFY NOTIFIABILITY. On receipt of the incident, the Director of Quality (Lisa McDonagh) in consultation with the Clinical Lead reviews the incident against the List of Notifiable Incidents in QA-13 Appendix 3 / Schedule 1 of the Patient Safety Act 2023. Document the determination with reasons.

Notify the Senior Accountable Officer (CEO Shauna Delaney) within 24 hours of incident occurrence per QA-13 Section 8.2. The HSE must also be informed within 24 hours of occurrence in order to gain assurance on immediate actions taken (QA-13 Section 8.2).
steps.role: Executive Management Team / Director of Quality

steps.action: WHO NOTIFIES. The CEO Shauna Delaney is responsible for ensuring the statutory notification is submitted, drawing on advice from the Director of Quality, Clinical Lead, William Fry legal counsel and HCI Quality Consultancy. The CEO cannot delegate statutory accountability.

STATUTORY WINDOW. Submit the statutory notification to the appropriate regulatory body within the statutory timeframe specified by the Patient Safety Act 2023 and any commencement orders issued. Home Support Services currently report notifiable incidents directly to the HSE under the HSE For-Profit Arrangement (QA-13 S
steps.role: Chief Executive Officer / Director of Quality
steps.tf: Within statutory window (default 7 days from CEO awareness pending finalised commencement)

steps.action: PREPARE THE NOTIFICATION. Include: incident identifier; date and time of occurrence and identification; location; description of what happened; description of harm or potential harm; details of the patient/Service User affected (with appropriate data protection — IM-05); immediate actions taken; identity of the notifie

Concurrent with the statutory notification, Liberty Living shall notify in line with QA-13 Section 7.11: HSE in writing without delay; An Garda Síochána where danger to staff/Service User or criminal offence suspected; Health and Safety Authority via IR1 form where dangerous occurrence or staff injury; Irish Medicines
steps.role: Chief Executive Officer / Director of Quality

steps.action: Commence the Open Disclosure Formal Meeting process under SOP-025 within 24-48 hours of the service becoming aware of the incident. Apply the statutory wording: the written record must state that the open disclosure was made pursuant to and in compliance with section 5(1) of the Patient Safety (Notifiable Incidents and
steps.role: Chief Executive Officer / Designated Liaison Person

steps.action: Convene the Serious Incident Management Team (SIMT) within 5 working days of SAO notification. Initiate a Systems Analysis review per QA-13 Section 8.0, targeting completion within 125 days from the time of occurrence.
steps.role: Senior Accountable Officer / Director of Quality
steps.tf: SIMT within 5 working days; Systems Analysis within 125 days

steps.action: Where the regulatory body requests further information, respond fully and promptly. Cooperate with any external review. Retain all records relating to the notification, the incident, the Open Disclosure process and the Systems Analysis review in a secure SharePoint folder per QA-13 Section 13.2.

Provide the Service User/relevant person and staff with a copy of the Systems Analysis review report through the Designated Liaison Person. Reiterate the apology and share learning (QA-13 Section 9.10).

Include the notifiable incident in the CEO's annual Open Disclosure Report per QA-13 Section 10.6, capturing the number of open disclosure events initiated and closed, the implementation of training, and evidence of staff support structures.

Record the notification, response and learning on the Liberty Living Risk Register and within the Clinical Governance Committee reporting cycle. Track corrective actions to completion and verify effectiveness through re-audit.
steps.tf: Until all corrective actions complete and verified

SOP-027.title: Recruit a New Staff Member (End-to-End)
SOP-027.purpose: To set out the end-to-end process Liberty Living shall follow when recruiting a new staff member — from identification of need through job specification, advertising, interview, qualification verification, Garda Vetting, reference checks, offer, contract, induction and probation — in compliance with HR-29 and HR-14, the HSE Authorisation Scheme V2.4 requirements, and the National Vetting Bureau Acts 2012-2016.

steps.action: IDENTIFY NEED. The CEO Shauna Delaney, in conjunction with the Director of HR (Claire Leavy), confirms the need to recruit based on workforce planning, the Staffing Action Plan, and the assessed needs of the Service User population. Document the rationale and the role required (full-time/part-time/weekend).
steps.role: Chief Executive Officer / Director of HR

steps.action: PREPARE JOB SPECIFICATION. For HCA roles, use the 'HCA Job Description' and 'HCA Job Spec' supporting documents. For other roles, develop in conjunction with the Executive Management Team. Specification must contain job title, grade, remuneration range, location, reporting relationships, principal duties, eligibility c
steps.role: Director of HR / Recruitment Manager Karen McLoughlin (085 801 7477)

steps.action: ADVERTISE. Apply open competition (HR-29 Section 6.2). Internal sources first where appropriate. External sources include recruitment agency, traditional/online advertising, social media, college partnerships, and the structured employee referral programme. Advertising must avoid discrimination of any kind under Employ
steps.tf: Within 10 working days of job spec approval

steps.action: RECEIVE AND SHORTLIST APPLICATIONS. All applicants submit up-to-date CV and Cover Letter to HR. Director of HR shortlists against criteria set in advance, strictly job-related. Maintain shortlisting records (criteria, scoring) for minimum 12 months (HR-29 Section 9.2-9.3).
steps.tf: Within 5 working days of closing date

steps.action: CONDUCT INTERVIEW. All candidates undergo a face-to-face interview assessing career history, employment gaps, and credentials. Use a structured Interview Plan and pre-defined competency criteria scored on a documented matrix. Panel composition reflects role level and gender balance where possible (HR-29 Section 10.0).

VERIFY QUALIFICATIONS & CREDENTIALS. For HCAs, verify: QQI Level 5 Major Award (or equivalent / commitment to obtain within 2 years per HR-29 Section 11.3-11.5); up-to-date Manual Handling and People Handling Certificate including hoist training; up-to-date Safeguarding and Children First certification; Infection P

OBTAIN AND CHECK TWO WRITTEN REFERENCES. Request two written references for every Home Support Worker applicant — from current and previous employers preferably. At least one reference verified by phone or face-to-face. Reference content covers work relationship, dates, duties, absenteeism, reason for leaving, qualific

VERIFY IDENTITY & INITIATE GARDA VETTING. Verify candidate identity using the An Garda Síochána 100-point check system including at least one photographic ID. Issue the Vetting Invitation Form. Manage the eVetting process through six steps: form to candidate, identity verification by HR Liaison, candidate completes
steps.tf: Before offer; vetting disclosure required pre-start

steps.action: OVERSEAS POLICE CLEARANCE. Where the candidate has resided outside Republic of Ireland for 6 months or more since age 16, obtain a Police Clearance Certificate from each country covering the entire residence period. Apply risk-management approach where clearance cannot be obtained (HR-29 Section 14.16-14.17).

RESPOND TO DISCLOSURES. Where vetting/disclosure identifies convictions or Specified Information, the CEO and Director of HR assess implications using a documented risk management approach (HR-29 Section 14.13). CEO determination of suitability is final. Where unsuitability is identified, communicate sensitively and af

OBTAIN MEDICAL CERTIFICATE & SUPPORTING DOCUMENTS. Obtain Medical Certificate of Fitness from a registered medical practitioner. Where required: Work permit; driving licence, car tax and insurance copies (where vehicle used for employment) (HR-29 Section 11.2).

ISSUE WRITTEN OFFER OF EMPLOYMENT. Make written offer once satisfactory references, qualifications, vetting and medical are in place. Candidate confirms acceptance in writing. Issue Contract of Employment from the 'Contracts - Liberty Living 2026' template no more than 5 days after commencement: full names, employer ad
steps.tf: Within 5 working days of recruitment decision; contract within 5 days of start

steps.action: COMMENCE INDUCTION (HR-14). Schedule induction prior to any care assignment. Provide Induction Schedule, Photo ID badge, uniform, Job Description, Careplanner App credentials, HSELanD account. Induction covers Liberty Living values/structure/services, statutory training (Children First, Data Protection, Safeguarding, C

COMPLETE SHADOWING AND NCCA. HCA completes a minimum 8 hours supervised shadowing during direct Service User contact across varying priority bands prior to undertaking any unsupervised care calls. CSM (QQI Level 6+) completes the National Carer Competency Assessment (NCCA) with the HCA against the HSE Home Support Auth

SOP-028.title: Receive and Manage a Protected Disclosure
SOP-028.purpose: To set out how Liberty Living receives, assesses, manages and provides feedback on a Protected Disclosure under the Protected Disclosures Act 2014 (as amended by the Protected Disclosures (Amendment) Act 2022), maintaining confidentiality of the reporting person, protecting them from penalisation, and ensuring timely follow-up.

steps.action: RECEIVE THE DISCLOSURE. The Designated Person receives the report verbally, in writing, by email or via the QA-37 Appendix 1 form. Where verbal, the Designated Person takes minutes and asks the worker to sign these where possible to confirm accuracy (QA-37 Section 11.6).
ACKNOWLEDGE RECEIPT. Acknowledge receipt of the disclosure to the reporting person within 7 calendar days (QA-37 Section 11.7).

PROTECT IDENTITY AND CONFIDENTIALITY. Treat the report in the strictest confidence per QA-37 Section 7.0. Do not disclose the reporting person's identity except where: necessary and proportionate under EU/Irish law; authorised by the reporter; required by exceptional public-interest, security, safety, environmental or

DO NOT INVESTIGATE INDEPENDENTLY. The reporter shall not investigate the wrongdoing themselves. The Designated Person retains responsibility for follow-up. Cooperation with any subsequent investigation is required but does not involve the reporter performing investigative steps (QA-37 Section 11.2; 12.13).

INITIAL ASSESSMENT. Carry out an initial assessment to determine whether the disclosure comes within the scope of a protected disclosure: (a) information came to the worker in a work-related context; (b) reasonable belief that the information tends to show relevant wrongdoing (QA-37 Section 11.8; Appendix 3). Where unc

ROUTING DECISION. If the matter relates solely to a complaint exclusively affecting the reporter, manage via Complaints Policy (QA-03). If there is no prima facie evidence of wrongdoing, close with rationale. If prima facie evidence exists, route the wrongdoing for investigation per QA-13 Incident Management Policy or

PROTECT FROM PENALISATION. Ensure the reporter and any facilitator, connected person, or owned entity are protected from any form of penalisation per QA-37 Section 9.0 — including suspension, demotion, unfair treatment, discipline, coercion, intimidation, harassment, discrimination, withholding of training, blacklistin

WRITTEN FEEDBACK WITHIN 3 MONTHS. Provide written feedback to the reporting person within 3 months of acknowledgement (or sooner where possible), and every 3 months thereafter while investigation continues. Feedback consists of information on action taken or envisaged. Feedback shall NOT include: information prejudicin
steps.tf: Within 3 months of acknowledgement; every 3 months thereafter

steps.action: INVESTIGATE THE WRONGDOING. Direct the wrongdoing to the appropriate Liberty Living process: QA-13 incident management; HS-23.1 allegations of abuse; HR-19 disciplinary; safeguarding via SOP-022. Investigators must be sufficiently independent. Conduct investigation per natural justice and due process. Maintain confiden
steps.role: Director of Quality and Compliance / Investigation Lead

steps.action: FINAL OUTCOME COMMUNICATION. The Designated Person completes a full investigation report and provides written details of the final outcome to the reporting person (QA-37 Section 11.12). Where appropriate, identify actions taken or to be taken and whether the alleged wrongdoings have been upheld, partly upheld or not up

ANONYMOUS DISCLOSURES. Where the disclosure is anonymous, follow up to the greatest extent possible. Anonymous reporters are still entitled to protections under the Act where the disclosure meets the Act's requirements. Acknowledge feedback may be limited (QA-37 Section 8.0).

GOVERNANCE OVERSIGHT AND AUDIT. The Director of Quality and Compliance monitors trends arising from disclosures, ensures organisational learning, and reports findings to the Executive Management Team and Clinical Governance Committee. Regular audits ensure compliance with the Act and the policy. Findings inform staff t

SOP-029.title: Handle a Subject Access Request (SAR)
SOP-029.purpose: To set out how Liberty Living receives, verifies, processes and responds to a Subject Access Request (SAR) under Article 15 of the General Data Protection Regulation (EU 2016/679), the Data Protection Acts 1988-2018 and Liberty Living IM-05, so that data subjects receive a copy of their personal data within the statutory window and any exemptions are applied lawfully.

steps.action: RECEIVE THE REQUEST. The DPO receives the request — verbal or written. Personal information shall never be provided over the phone (IM-05 Section 13.3). Log the request in the SAR register with timestamp.

VERIFY IDENTITY. Where appropriate, verify the requester's identity using 'reasonable means' per IM-05 Section 13.4. Where doubt exists about identity, request evidence proportionate to the sensitivity of the data and risk of disclosure to a third party.

ACKNOWLEDGE THE REQUEST. Acknowledge the SAR in writing, confirming receipt, the 30 calendar day response timeframe (Article 15 GDPR), and any clarification required. Where Liberty Living holds a large quantity of information, ask the data subject to specify the information sought (IM-05 Section 13.9).

ASSESS WHETHER ANY EXEMPTIONS APPLY per IM-05 Section 13.14: prevention/detection of offences; estimate of damages in a claim against Liberty Living; legal professional privilege; statistics/research; back-up data; serious harm to physical/mental health (S.I. 82/1989); disclosure of data about another individual withou
steps.role: Data Protection Officer / DPO with legal counsel

steps.action: COMPILE THE DATA. Search and retrieve all personal data relating to the data subject across CRM, paper files, email, HR records, training records, complaints log, safeguarding log, incident reports, financial records, and Data Processor systems. Keep a record of search efforts in case of complaint to the Data Protectio

WORK WITH DATA PROCESSORS. Where any data is held by a Data Processor (e.g. Careplanner CRM provider, payroll bureau, IT host), engage them via the Data Processing Agreement to source the requested information (IM-05 Section 13.10).

REDACT THIRD-PARTY DATA. Conduct a redaction exercise to remove any information about other individuals before information is sent to the data subject (IM-05 Section 13.5). Where the third party has consented, that information may be included. Where personal data consists of an expression of opinion given in confidence

PREPARE THE DISCLOSURE PACK. Compile a clearly explained pack including: confirmation that personal data is being processed; a copy of the personal data; categories of personal data processed and purposes; identity of recipients (those to whom data has been disclosed); source of data unless contrary to public interest;

ISSUE THE RESPONSE WITHIN 30 CALENDAR DAYS of receipt (Article 15 GDPR). Where the request is electronic, provide in a commonly used electronic format. Where by post, use registered post, double-wrapped and marked confidential (IM-05 Section 13.6). Free of charge as a rule.

EXTENSION WHERE NECESSARY. Where the request is complex or there are numerous requests, the DPO may extend the period by up to 60 further calendar days (i.e. up to 90 days total). The DPO must inform the data subject within 30 days of receipt and explain why the extension is necessary (IM-05 Section 13.8).
steps.tf: Within 30 days of receipt if extension applied

steps.action: MANIFESTLY UNFOUNDED OR EXCESSIVE REQUESTS. Where the request is manifestly unfounded or excessive (particularly repetitive), the DPO may either charge a reasonable fee (administrative cost basis) or refuse to respond. Where refusing, provide justification, inform the data subject of their right to complain to the DPC,

RECORD AND CLOSE. Record the request, response, search efforts and any exemptions on the SAR register. Retain documentation for the period required for audit and DPC complaint defence per IM-05 Section 17.0-18.0.

POST-RESPONSE SUPPORT. Where the data subject has follow-up questions or concerns regarding the response, direct them to the DPO. Where the data subject is dissatisfied, advise them of their right to complain to the Data Protection Commission (DPC) and the Liberty Living Complaints Policy (QA-03).

SOP-030.title: Activate Business Continuity Plan
SOP-030.purpose: To set out how Liberty Living activates its Business Continuity Plan (BCP) in response to an emergency or disruptive event, so that critical care is maintained for high-priority Service Users, staff are supported, communications are coordinated, and statutory notifications are made — in line with GOV-11.

steps.action: CONVENE CRISIS COMMAND. Activate the Crisis Command structure per GOV-11 Section 6.14: CEO as Incident Lead (strategic oversight); Director of Operations coordinates operational response; Clinical Lead provides clinical oversight; administration team supports communications with Service Users, families, staff and exter

RETRIEVE BCP. Retrieve the Business Continuity Folder from the off-site secure location, which contains: the Directory of Contacts; the Business Continuity Plan; a copy of GOV-11; and access arrangements for critical Service User information to ensure continuity of care where electronic systems are unavailable (GOV-11

PRIORITISE SERVICE USERS. The Director of Operations, in consultation with the CEO and Clinical Lead, prioritises continuity of care for Service Users with highest dependency or clinical risk — Priority 1 essential personal care, medication prompting, safeguarding monitoring, welfare checks (GOV-11 Section 6.4). Where

DEPLOY SKELETON STAFF. Activate the rota of skeleton staff and reassign HCAs to priority calls. The Director of Operations ensures immediate support is provided to HCAs and other staff including out-of-hours support. Accurate and up-to-date information is handed over to and from HCAs and on-call staff (GOV-11 Section 6

COMMUNICATE WITH SERVICE USERS AND FAMILIES. Administration staff maintain regular contact with all Service Users or their family members/representatives during the emergency event. Inform of any temporary adjustments to services as soon as possible (GOV-11 Section 6.12.1).

COMMUNICATE WITH STAFF. Communicate with on-duty staff via email, phone, group cascade. Communicate with off-duty staff via SMS, phone, WhatsApp, local media, and social media. Ensure all staff understand their roles in the BCP response (GOV-11 Section 6.12.2-6.12.3).
steps.role: Chief Executive Officer / Director of Operations

steps.action: NOTIFY HSE EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT. The CEO ensures the HSE Emergency Management office is informed of the emergency event, the proposed response per the BCP, and the status of its ongoing implementation. Liberty Living provides the HSE with a statement of missed calls and reasons under the Service Arrangement / Scheme re
steps.tf: As early as practicable; ongoing updates

steps.action: NOTIFY INSURERS. In the event of an emergency the CEO shall, without delay, contact the company insurance brokers — the event may be an insured risk (GOV-11 Section 5.4).

MAINTAIN EMERGENCY EVENT LOG. Throughout the response, the CEO documents and maintains a log of actions and decisions made — including ref, date, time, details, action/decision, assigned to, and status (GOV-11 Section 6.11.3 / Appendix 3).

MANAGE INCIDENTS DURING THE EMERGENCY. Where the emergency results in harm, risk of harm or disruption to Service Users, manage the event in accordance with QA-13 Incident Management and Open Disclosure Policy in addition to the BCP. Open Final Incident Reports and convene SIMT for any Category 1 incidents (GOV-11 Sect

RED WEATHER WARNING — SPECIFIC ACTIONS. For Status Red severe weather, follow GOV-11 Section 6.6: review HSE Severe Weather Checklist Response Phase; consider relevant actions; ensure staff awareness of Met Éireann National Weather Warning System and consequences; apply HSE Adverse Weather Policy payment provisions to
steps.role: Chief Executive Officer / Director of HR

steps.action: DEACTIVATE BCP. When the emergency has passed, the CEO deactivates the BCP and communicates to all staff and stakeholders. Restore normal scheduling and operations.

LESSONS LEARNED REVIEW. Carry out a Lessons Learned review of the emergency event. Ensure corrective actions are implemented from lessons learned. Highlight previous positive outcomes for future reference. Update the BCP to reflect learning (GOV-11 Section 6.13.1).
steps.role: Chief Executive Officer / Director of Operations

SOP-031.title: Schedule First Visit and Brief Healthcare Assistant
SOP-031.purpose: To ensure that, once a Home Support Care Plan is approved, the first visit to a new Service User is safely scheduled and the allocated Healthcare Assistant (HCA) is properly briefed on the Care Plan, routines, risks and contingency arrangements before crossing the threshold of the Service User's home.

steps.action: Confirm staffing capacity for the proposed schedule before committing the package; verify no immediate-family allocation conflict exists and that hours do not breach the Organisation of Working Time Act 1997.
steps.role: Director of HR (Claire Leavy) / Client Services Manager
steps.tf: Before HCA is offered the package

steps.action: Allocate the named HCA in the CRM based on competencies, skills, geographic coverage and continuity of care; record the allocation decision and rationale.
steps.tf: Within 1 working day of Care Plan sign-off

steps.action: Schedule the first visit in consultation with the Service User and/or family, taking account of their preferences; confirm date, time and duration in the CRM and issue confirmation to the Service User.

Push a digital copy of the Home Support Care Plan, Schedule of Services, Getting to Know Me profile and all current risk assessments (Environmental, Falls, Medication) to the HCA via the CRM mobile app.
steps.tf: At least 24 hours before first visit

steps.action: Hold a verbal handover briefing with the HCA covering: Service User routines and preferences, identified risks and Risk Management Plan, manual handling needs, medication prompting (where applicable), safeguarding flags, lone-worker arrangements and refusal-of-care protocol.

Issue the HCA with the on-call contact list, Liberty Living Welcome Letter for the Service User, ID badge confirmation, Home Record Sheet (CARE-08 Appendix 13) and the Liberty Living complaints contact card (Leah O'Brien, complaints@libertyhomecare.ie, 01 416 3717).

Attend the first visit (Client Services Manager or Supervisor) with the HCA where reasonably practicable; introduce the HCA to the Service User, confirm the Care Plan, agree the schedule and address any discrepancies or concerns on the spot.
Verify the HCA begins documentation in the home care diary and CRM record sheet at the first call (arrival/departure, tasks delivered, observations).

Conduct settling-in check-in calls with both the Service User and the HCA at 48 hours, 1 week and 2 weeks; address any issues immediately and refer to Clinical Lead (Lisa McDonagh) for Care Plan adjustments.
At end of the settling-in period, confirm Care Plan is operating as intended; flag any required amendments for Clinical Lead approval and update CRM accordingly.

SOP-032.title: Conduct an Interim / Triggered Care Plan Review
SOP-032.purpose: To ensure that an unscheduled (triggered) reassessment of a Service User's Home Support Care Plan is initiated within 48 hours and completed within 10 days of a triggering event, so that care delivery remains safe, person-centred and responsive to changing needs.

steps.action: Capture the trigger event in the CRM within the same working day, recording who reported it, what occurred, date/time and immediate risk indicators.

Apply initial risk screening; where there is immediate clinical risk or deterioration, contact the Clinical Lead (Lisa McDonagh, 085 800 3961, Lisa@libertyhomecare.ie) for professional oversight and any interim safety measures.
Initiate the unscheduled review within 48 hours of the trigger; contact the Service User and/or representative to arrange a home visit, explaining purpose, and seek consent under CARE-06.

Conduct the home visit and complete the Client Review (Appendix 12) covering: current health and functional status, ADLs, equipment, medication, environmental factors, safeguarding indicators, support network and Service User preferences and goals.

Where the review indicates an alteration to the level or type of support is required, document the recommendation, obtain Clinical Lead approval and (for HSE-funded Service Users) submit to the HSE for review and decision.

Amend the Home Support Care Plan and Schedule of Services only within the context of this review; obtain Service User consent (or Decision Supporter agreement) to all changes; have Clinical Lead sign off.
Communicate the updated Care Plan to the allocated HCA(s) via CRM mobile app and verbal handover; place a copy in the Service User's home folder and notify family/representatives where consented.
Issue written notification of the outcome of the review to the Service User and their representative within 10 working days of determination.
Where a Quality Improvement Plan or risk register update is warranted (e.g. recurrent falls, safeguarding pattern), refer to SOP-037 (Open a QIP) and SOP-043 (Add a New Risk).
steps.role: Client Services Manager / Director of Quality (Lisa McDonagh)
steps.tf: Within 5 working days of review close

SOP-033.title: Service Termination / Discharge
SOP-033.purpose: To set out a consistent, auditable process for ending the home support relationship with a Service User - whether by Service User choice, transfer to another setting, bereavement, or Liberty Living withdrawal - in a way that maintains continuity of care, protects records and complies with HSE notification obligations.

steps.action: Record the discharge trigger in the CRM, identifying the route (Service User-initiated, transfer, bereavement, Liberty Living withdrawal), date notified and source of notification.

For Liberty Living-initiated withdrawal, escalate to Director of Operations (Lisa McDonagh); confirm reason (e.g. inability to safely staff; criminal activity, anti-social behaviour, bullying or harassment of staff); engage the local HSE Home Support Manager case-by-case.

For non-exceptional Liberty Living withdrawal from HSS, provide at least three (3) months' written notice to the HSE, including proposals for hand-back of the Home Support package to ensure continuity and least disruption.
Notify the Service User and/or their representative in writing of the discharge date, reasons (where appropriate), and onward signposting or referral options; offer a final visit to close out.
Where the Service User is being transferred to another provider or setting, coordinate handover under GOV-36 (multi-agency / discharge coordination); share relevant care information with consent.

Plan and deliver the final visit: complete final Home Record Sheet entry, collect Liberty Living equipment / keys / paperwork, leave Service User Home Folder with the Service User or representative as appropriate, and complete final reconciliation of any monies or property held (CARE-17).
Close the Service User in the CRM: change status to Discharged with date and reason; ensure all care records, risk assessments, complaints history and incident records are complete.
steps.tf: Within 5 working days of final visit

steps.action: Archive the Service User physical file in line with IM-30 Record Keeping retention periods; restrict access to authorised staff only; confirm digital archive in CRM.
steps.tf: Within 10 working days of CRM closure

steps.action: For HSE-funded Service Users, confirm the discharge to the HSE in writing and update the Approved Provider portal records; submit any outstanding monthly invoices with missed-call statement under GOV-25.
steps.tf: By the next monthly HSE submission cycle

steps.action: Conduct a brief internal close-out review of the discharge with the Client Services Manager and Clinical Lead (Lisa McDonagh): identify any learning, complaints, safeguarding or QIP triggers; record in CRM and refer to SOP-037 if a QIP is warranted.
steps.tf: Within 10 working days of CRM closure

steps.action: Re-deploy the allocated HCA(s) to maintain continuity of employment and notify them of the closure, including any debrief required following a bereavement.
steps.role: Director of HR (Claire Leavy) / Client Services Manager
steps.tf: Within 5 working days of final visit

SOP-034.title: Plan and Schedule an Audit
SOP-034.purpose: To establish a standardised process for developing, approving and communicating Liberty Living's annual Quality Audit Schedule so that audit coverage is risk-based, evidence-based and aligned with HSE specifications, HSE KPIs, legislation and the eight themes of the National Standards.

steps.action: Map the audit universe against HSE Specifications, HSE KPIs, legislation, the eight National Standards themes, the Risk Registers and high-risk processes (medication, safeguarding, manual handling, lone-worker, IPC, records).

Draft the annual Quality Audit Schedule using the LLH Audit Calendar 2 template, detailing for each audit: scope, applicable policies/forms, Auditor, Audit Owner, target month/quarter and risk rationale.
Allocate Auditors ensuring independence from the activity being audited; confirm competence and approve any external contractor auditor before use.
Submit the draft schedule to the Director of Operations (Lisa McDonagh) for approval, confirming audits are appropriate and relevant.
Communicate the approved schedule to all Auditors and Audit Owners via email and in EMT meeting; publish to the QMS shared drive; brief any new Auditors on the process.
Submit the annual Quality Audit programme to the HSE in accordance with GOV-36 / QA-22.1 s.5.5 (Director of Operations responsibility).
steps.tf: By end of January each year

steps.action: Monitor adherence to the schedule monthly through the EMT pack (SOP-040); flag any slippage, re-prioritise where risk profile changes, and update the LLH Audit Calendar with version control.

SOP-035.title: Conduct an Audit using the Audit Working Pack
SOP-035.purpose: To ensure every internal quality audit is conducted in a standardised, evidence-based and independent manner using the Liberty Living Audit Working Pack, in line with the seven audit principles of QA-22.1 (integrity, fair presentation, due professional care, confidentiality, independence, evidence-based and risk-based approach).

steps.action: Auditor contacts the Audit Owner to schedule the audit and agree an Audit Plan covering date/time, scope, requirements, sampling approach and any pre-audit documentation requests.
steps.tf: At least 2 weeks before audit date

steps.action: Pre-audit document review: examine prior audit reports and QIPs, previous external findings, related incidents, complaints, KPIs and risks listed for the audit area; complete an audit checklist where applicable.
Hold the Opening Meeting with the Audit Owner (and Line Manager of the area where different): clarify the structure of the audit, walk through the Audit Plan and answer queries.
steps.tf: On audit day - start of audit

steps.action: Conduct a walk-around where applicable to observe the actual application of the process being audited; capture observations and any items for further investigation.
Interview relevant staff involved in the process (HCAs, Supervisors, Clinical Lead, Client Services Manager) and document the communications and evidence provided.

Sample Service User records and/or staff records: sample size proportionate to population and risk; selection random; sample reflects high and low support levels where Service User records are reviewed; anonymise references.

Maintain the Audit Trail Record (QA-22.1 Appendix 2) capturing all documents reviewed, standards referenced, staff interviewed, sample IDs, sample sizes and policy/form references with version numbers.
Identify findings against the audit criteria: areas of good practice, non-conformances and observations; cross-check against KPI targets where relevant.
Hold the Closing Meeting with the Audit Owner (and Line Manager where different): present findings, discuss and agree them; where agreement cannot be reached, document the disagreement.
Hand off to write-up (SOP-036) and confirm the timeline for issuing the Quality Audit Report; secure all working papers in the QMS shared drive with restricted access.

SOP-036.title: Write Up Audit Findings and Recommendations
SOP-036.purpose: To produce a Quality Audit Report that truthfully and accurately presents the findings, conclusions and recommendations of a completed internal quality audit, and that triggers the QIP process for any required corrective actions.

steps.action: Draft the Quality Audit Report in the template detailing: audit scope, requirements reviewed, documents reviewed, sample sizes, findings (with evidence), areas of good practice and any agreed obstacles or disagreements.
steps.tf: Within 5 working days of audit completion

steps.action: Anonymise all Service User and staff identifying details; reference Service Users only by anonymised IDs traceable in the secure audit working papers.
For each finding requiring corrective action, classify as a non-conformance and assess severity using the HSE Risk Scoring Matrix (likelihood x impact) to assign High / Medium / Low.
Formulate clear, actionable, SMART recommendations for each non-conformance, specifying the desired outcome (not the activity); record areas of good practice with supporting evidence.
